Unfortunately one of the early design constraints in gabbi was that it work properly with the kinds of test discovery and reporting that's used in the belly of openstack ci. The main implication of that is that before any tests actually run they are _all_ created. You can see this in the way that the load_tests method creates a suite of TestSuites. When being used with `testr` the list of tests is discovered, split for concurrency, and then fed back as patterns into the test discovery process. It gets a bit confused and confusing.

Does that mean an iterator that can be interrupted is not an option? No. Just that we need to be aware of the constraints that come about by being somewhat wed to testr+subunit and that failure handling is different depending on whether it is failure in creating a test or in running the test.

Note: When I discovered all this (what felt like) chaos it was after coming from what felt like the much purer world of py.test and it made me shake my head. Alot.

As you can see `glFramebufferTexture` is `nil` as well as the internal glew symbol for `glClearColor`. `lib.c` would break the moment you use anything but OpenGL 1.0.

You can work around this by initializing OpenGL (glad or glew) in your library:

```c
void lib_init() 
{
#ifndef USE_GLEW
    gladLoadGL();
#endif
}
```
```c
    // Dynamically load a function from lib.so
    void *lib_code = dlopen("./lib.so", RTLD_LAZY | RTLD_DEEPBIND);
    draw_blue_screen_func *draw_blue_screen = (draw_blue_screen_func *) dlsym(lib_code, "draw_blue_screen");
    ((void(*)()) dlsym(lib_code, "lib_init"))();
```

I don't know if you can do some tricks and force the library to use the already loaded symbols of your application, it may work if you dynamically link against glad/glew as a dynamic library in both files (`main.c` and `lib.c`) but honestly I am not familiar enough with POSIX nor dynamic linking.

I hope that answers your question.

May be we should be talking at the selector level.
Scenario is as follow:

- There is a socket.
- Its "Read" event is registered to the selector. (Registered by SSL read)
- Now an other "Read" registration request arrives. (From SSL Write)
- What happens?

> I could possibly envision some kind of hybrid approach that allows a certain small number of eager reads to take place first before blocking on the event loop. That might allow more normal workloads to take advantage of eager read/write while forcing the extreme case to more properly schedule itself.

First, I would agree, that given the low probability of this phenomenon to occur, the hybrid approach should work.

Second, I think, this issue could be resolved on the application level. Let the user decide, whether to use the eager r/w optimization, or to guard himself against such a DoS. To do this, user can explicitly tell the socket to receive only **_at most_** some number of bytes, and than voluntarily call ```await sleep(0)```. We can even introduce a special ```sleep(0)``` trap alias, like ```await switch()```. Similarly, when sending some big amount data, user can break it into some reasonably sized pieces:

```python
async def send(sock, data, chunk_size=1000):
    totalsent = 0
    while totalsent < len(data):
        sent = await self.sock.send(data[totalsent:totalsent + chunk_size])
        totalsent = totalsent + sent
        await switch()
```
The only thing we should definitely do is to stress the eager r/w behavior in the documentation, maybe even with the example solution to this corner case.

Where, precisely, have I ever said that Curio is something that I don't intend to maintain?

Apparently it must be some kind of impossible to understand mental concept that Curio can remain a small, very specific library, that stays focused on what it's doing right now.  Or that I need to be sitting here adding feature after feature upon feature.  Not everything needs to turn into some kind of giant framework from hell.  Not every open source project needs a freaking laptop sticker and a conference dedicated to it. 

Did I write Curio to be a replacement for something like Twisted?  No I didn't.  Do I want it to be usable and reliable for what it is?  You bet.   I'm certainly open to any bug reports and pull requests that fix bugs and improve its documentation.    However, if people are sitting around waiting for me to patch in some kind of turn-key HTTP/2 support, they're going to be waiting a long time.   Curio is a building block for creating other interesting things--not an all-inclusive framework that attempts to solve every possible problem involving I/O.  I'm happy with it being exactly that.
 
That said, I'll be pushing a new release fairly soon. Bug reports welcome ;-)

Ah, I see the issue. Unfortunately, it is very difficult to fix it. When you have a huge reference database, minimap2 only holds part of it in memory, aligns all reads against this part and then move to the next part of the reference. Because it is unable to see all the reference sequences before doing alignment, it can't write the right SAM header.

There are two solutions to this. 1) you can increase option `-I` such that minimap2 can load all references into memory:
```sh
minimap2 -a -I100g ref.fa reads.fa
```
You will need a machine with huge RAM for this. 2) you can filter out all SAM header lines and then add them when you convert SAM to BAM:
```sh

I found the root cause of this. The language metadata extractor was implemented incorrectly back in #50. The language field in the RDF is typed as RFC 4646 so we need to reflect this in the extractor.

Example RDF:

```xml
<dcterms:language>
  <rdf:Description rdf:nodeID="Ndc98bbe6c7bb4281b75c633906a61a97">
    <rdf:value rdf:datatype="http://purl.org/dc/terms/RFC4646">nl</rdf:value>
  </rdf:Description>
</dcterms:language>
```

The important part here is the `datatype="http://purl.org/dc/terms/RFC4646"`. We'll need to specify this when converting the queried value into RDF. This can be done via the datatype argument to the [Literal constructor](http://rdflib.readthedocs.io/en/stable/rdf_terms.html#literals).

@lardconcepts I'm not sure I see your point...

Just to provide some context: when you call an OWM API endpoint you get data that is referred - as you stated - to a UNIX timestamp: this is referred to as `reference_time` in PyOWM, and means the point in time when the measurement was done by OWM data sourcers. Many PyOWM entity objects also carry `reception_time`, that is the UNIX timestamp when the entity object was created.

So, PyOWM is just giving you the value for `reference_time` as it is returned by the API - if the API sends back a wrong value (meaning: you expected that value to be referring to a different day of the week), the problem is on the API side and in that case I would suggest you to notify the API owners.

Also notice that before version 2.5 PyOWM used to provide datetime objects that were *not* timezone-aware. Is this your case?

Did I - at least partially - shed light on your doubt?

Also, FYI, instead of:

    datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(int(weather.get_reference_time())).strftime('%A')

you can use

    weather.get_reference_time(timeformat='date').strftime('%A')

Cheers!

I have also been looking for a disconnect event or a connection status that can be polled. I have a RPi3 communicating to a JNHuaMao HM-10 serial-to-BLE module. Ideally they would stay connected forever. However the RPi acting as the central, needs to re-establish the connection to the peripheral device after any connection breaks. The RPi is running Jessie and I have installed BlueZ 5.42.

Here is the "connect/receive some data/peripheral-disconnect" sequence using gatttool interactively.
First, I call the tool and make the connection. Notice how the mac address changes colour (bolded here) after the successful connection:

pi@NigelPi:~/BLEtest $ sudo gatttool -b D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32 -I
[D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32][LE]> connect
Attempting to connect to D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32
Connection successful
[**D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32**][LE]>

Now some data is received and is printed as a notification. The coloured mac address remains in the prompt:

pi@NigelPi:~/BLEtest $ sudo gatttool -b D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32 -I
[D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32][LE]> connect
Attempting to connect to D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32
Connection successful
Notification handle = 0x0012 value: 48 65 6c 6c 6f 20 77 6f 72 6c 64
[**D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32**][LE]>

The peripheral is now turned off. There is no notification in the user interface. However, if the Enter key is pressed, the  prompt is over-written with plain text. This has to be seen to be believed!

pi@NigelPi:~/BLEtest $ sudo gatttool -b D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32 -I
[D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32][LE]> connect
Attempting to connect to D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32
Connection successful
Notification handle = 0x0012 value: 48 65 6c 6c 6f 20 77 6f 72 6c 64
[D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32][LE]>

So gatttool knows about the disconnection, but requires prompting to display it. There is no specific event. Hence in the following code, the final while loop must either receive an exception, or include some status polling, so that it can be aware of the disconnect.

#!/usr/bin/env python

# Connect to HM-10 BLE in peripheral mode and listen for incoming
# data. Peripheral will disconnect after all data sent.

from __future__ import print_function

import logging
logging.basicConfig()
logging.getLogger('pygatt').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

import pygatt
import time
import sys

address = "D0:B5:C2:9F:8F:32"

adapter = pygatt.GATTToolBackend()
adapter.start()

while True:
   try:
      print("Connecting...")
      device = adapter.connect(address,3,pygatt.BLEAddressType.public)
      print("Connected")
      break
   except pygatt.exceptions.NotConnectedError:
      print("Not connected")
      time.sleep(1)
      continue

#device.subscribe('0000ffe1-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb', callback = printNotification)

while True:
   try:
      time.sleep(1)
   except Exception as e:
      print(e)

After thinking about this, I'd prefer to not have the specific use-case be built-in to `gffutils`.  It would be better off living in a separate package that simply imports `gffutils` and has the functionality you're looking for.

I do want to make sure `gfftutils` has the general-purpose pieces in place to do what you need, so I've added `FeatureDB.delete` and the `biopython_integration` module (87c2a37d440b2231778a4970e8e063f651405a80 and f82fd2e8c4f617e36ac761614afba0cf7ac63da8) to be able to do things you describe in the gist.

The general workflow would be to write iterators for things you want to add and things you want to remove, and then use `FeatureDB.delete` and `FeatureDB.update` with them.  Here are some example building blocks:


```python
# -----------------------------------------------------
# Update a database with features from a GenBank file
#
import gffutils.biopython_integration as bpi
def gen():
    "yields gffutils.Feature objects from a genbank file"
    for rec in Bio.SeqIO.parse('ex.gb', 'genbank'):
        for seqfeature in rec.features:
            yield bpi.from_seqfeature(seqfeature)

db.update(gen())

# -----------------------------------------------------
# Delete features from within a region
#
db.delete(db.region('scaffold_13:271666-273390'))


# -----------------------------------------------------
# Shift the start/stop coords of features by the deletion size
#
def gen2(features, n):
    "yields features with coords shifted by n bp"
    for feature in features:
        feature.start += n
        feature.stop += n
        yield feature

# select features that come after the deletion region
features_to_adjust = db.region('scaffold_13:273390-99999999')

# adjust 'em
n = 271666 - 273390
adjusted_features = gen2(features_to_adjust, n)

# update db, replacing existing features with new adjusted ones
db.update(adjusted_features, merge_strategy='replace')
```

It should be noted the vector code and the intrinsics work much the same,  actually I did a very quick check (on power8) and came out with the exact same code.   On a larger example I'd expect the compiler may have better optimization opportunities,  either way there really won't be much difference.  I'm doing it this way because it makes the most sense,  as it's the most compatible and the way gcc is moving forward with simd code across multiple architectures.

Re: the directory idea. Would it be possible to point a `session` object at a responses directory, and have clients created from that session inherit it? This is how I'm envisioning it:

Say I have an `assume_role` function that can be passed a session as one of its arguments - for example, maybe I want to use an MFA session that I've previously established. I pass it a session that has Placebo attached to it, pointed to `./mocks`. The `assume_role` function creates an STS client from the passed session; any calls to that session then check in ./mocks/sts for saved responses before making the call to botocore. Now I have a way to tell my function to use mocked STS calls instead of real ones - just modify the session it uses, which lives outside the function scope

Or, on the opposite end: Say I'm using a single session for my entire application, or just the default boto3 session. In my code, I add a flag to attach placebo to the session. When placebo is attached (in 'save' mode), everything runs exactly the same, except that every client created from the session automatically stores every response to every call in the appropriate `./mocks/...` subdirectory. Now I can build mocked responses for an entire execution by only modifying one small part of the code - essentially adding a single 'record' button.

The error is a red herring; here's what's happening.

`blob.pos_tags` returns pos tags using the Penn Treebank tagset, but the WordNet Lemmatizer expects the WordNet tagset.  When you call the `lemmatize` method with the Treebank tag, NLTK throws a KeyError which bubbles up as missing corpora.

To workaround, use the `lemma` property, which automatically converts the tags:
```python
print("{w}: {p}".format(w=w.lemma, p=pos))
```

I'll submit a PR to automatically convert the tags when using `lemmatize` as well.

TextBlob is training the classifier on each iteration of the loop.  Try creating an instance of Blobber once (which invokes training), and then run it against each string of text in your loop.

Like this:

```python
from textblob.sentiments import NaiveBayesAnalyzer
from textblob import Blobber
import re

data = open('reviews.csv', 'r').readlines()
blobber = Blobber(analyzer=NaiveBayesAnalyzer())

for row in data:
    text = re.sub('[^\w\s'+'.'+']', '', row)

    blob = blobber(text) 
    result = [text, blob.sentiment.classification]
    print result
```

Okay, so I gave this some thought.

Sub-classing future seems wrong to me.
Adding new functions for the select behavior makes more sense then touching the existing ones, but again, there is really no need for it to be in the channel itself.

And i still don't understand the actual need for the channel to be returned.
You'd need to inspect the returned `ch` from a select before you'd know what could
even be in the second part of the returned tuple. Either a response from put or a response from get.
It all seems very non-pythonic.

`aiochannel` is pretty much _just_ a "closable queue", and I don't want to add too many extras.

That said, wrapping the behavior in an external module should be fairly easy.
You can build select-like behavior without touching the `aiochannel` package at all.
Cancelling a pending `get()` or `put()` future should prevent it from happening (given that it is not already `completed()` ofc :smile:)

Your pychan example could be implemented that way:
```python
await select([ch1], [(ch2, 'item')])
```

I'd be okay with merging something like that as a separate module, so it would be used something like

```python
from aiochannel import Channel, select
ch1 = Channel()
ch2 = Channel()

ch, val = await select([ch1], [(ch2, "item")])
```

> Even though I also would generally prefer being consistent across all functions, I think this is a special case, since music segmentation is an evolving task (that has never really been formally defined),

So what should we do, have two separate `evaluate` functions in `segment`?  Or just make the hierarchical eval function an orphan function, not included in `evaluate`?  If this metric is evolving/not formally defined, we probably should wait to include it.  

> we should be a bit more flexible allowing cases like the hierarchical evaluation taking custom parameters.

The issue isn't that it takes custom parameters (most metrics do), it's that its call spec is incompatible with the other metrics in `segment` (as far as I've deduced, correct me if I'm wrong)

> The issue here is that the window and hop parameters are application dependent, not just sample rate dependent.

Following convention here, we should have multiple functions with sensible defaults for each application.  `mir_eval` tries to implement community-standard metrics, and that standardization includes parameterization.  If there are no agreed-upon parameters in the community, that's somewhat problematic.

> This evaluation method would likely be used by those with knowledge of source separation and the meaning of the metrics they would receive.

We can't really make this assumption - while I would expect people calling this function to at least know what a window and hop size are, I wouldn't expect them to know the conventions as far as how to set them (and in different applications).  I mean, I have worked on source separation a bit, and I would not know what the standard setting for them was in pretty much any application.


All this being said, I recognize that source separation is a bit different from the rest of `mir_eval`, so if there is general agreement that having default parameter values for the hop and window doesn't make any sense, I'm happy to defer to community judgement.

Hi @unnir 

You sometimes run into this issue with random forest classes because they are not fully thread safe. Scikit-learn have [fixed](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/2755) this but it's not part of their conda build yet. Should be in their next update cycle.

Typically, you get this error if your code produces a warning somewhere before the ``fit`` or ``predict`` call, but I'm not sure why yet. If your code does print warnings, try silencing them.

Alternatively, you can use multiprocessing as the backend, set ``backend='multiprocessing'`` when you instantiate an ensemble. If you always want multiprocessing, set it as an environmental variable:

```bash
export MLENS_BACKEND='multiprocessing'
```


Note that while multiprocessing works for Scikit-learn estimators, you may have issues with third-party packages like xgboost and lgbm. If you include these in the ensemble, you typically need to use ``'threading'`` as the backend. 

Hope this helps, let me know otherwise.

@avinash-mishra I do agree that documentation is lacking, but http://kafka-python.readthedocs.io/en/master/usage.html#kafkaconsumer should give you some start. The proper setup is always in a loop with `poll()`:
```python
from kafka import KafkaConsumer

# To consume latest messages and auto-commit offsets
consumer = KafkaConsumer('my-topic',
                         group_id='my-group',
                         bootstrap_servers=['localhost:9092'])
while True:
    # Response format is {TopicPartiton('topic1', 1): [msg1, msg2]}
    msg_pack = consumer.poll(timeout_ms=500)

    for tp, messages in msg_pack:
        # message value and key are raw bytes -- decode if necessary!
        # e.g., for unicode: `message.value.decode('utf-8')`
        print ("%s:%d:%d: key=%s value=%s" % (tp.topic, tp.partition,
                                              message.offset, message.key,
                                              message.value))
```

or by using `for` iteration:
```python
from kafka import KafkaConsumer

# To consume latest messages and auto-commit offsets
consumer = KafkaConsumer('my-topic',
                         group_id='my-group',
                         bootstrap_servers=['localhost:9092'])
for message in consumer:
    # message value and key are raw bytes -- decode if necessary!
    # e.g., for unicode: `message.value.decode('utf-8')`
    print ("%s:%d:%d: key=%s value=%s" % (message.topic, message.partition,
                                          message.offset, message.key,
                                          message.value))
```

Fundamentally, this issue is the result of a bad API.

When passing arguments using `col=[v1, v2]`, the meaning of the argument can be one of either "find me a range of things between these two values" and "find me exactly these two values". This wouldn't be too bad, but "find me exactly two values" has a different underlying meaning depending on whether the index is a numeric index, unique index, prefix/suffix index, or full text index, or even in some cases when a column has more than one index (an individual column can have up to 4 indexes built on it to service different types of queries)!

For the short term, I'm going to fix the exception itself with a Lua script, but longer term I'm going to need to work on the API.

Hi @wanghaisheng, and thanks @jsfenfen. `pdfplumber` implements a version of Nurminen's approach in [`table.py`](https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber/blob/master/pdfplumber/table.py). One major difference, as @jsfenfen notes, is that Nurminen's original approach uses computer vision to detects edges, whereas `pdfplumber` the lines and character bounding boxes explicitly provided by fully-digital PDFs.

I'm working on a major update to `pdfplumber` that will change some aspects of how the user specifies which types of edges to use when finding tables. But the general approach — and, especially, the conversion of these edges to tables — will remain the same.

There is API documentation, though perhaps not as many examples as one would like: http://boofuzz.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#api-documentation

Different loggers are here: http://boofuzz.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/logging.html

As for how to use, see the `fuzz_data_logger` parameter for `Session` here: http://boofuzz.readthedocs.io/en/latest/source/Session.html

It would probably be best to add a note about logging to the Quickstart guide. Also, the Session object API docs make it seem like you need to pass a `FuzzLogger` but I _think_ you can pass any `IFuzzLogger`.

2 things are slowing down the process:

1. `sct_img.rgb` which is a on demand attribute. It means that its value is computed only when required, and computed one time only.
2. `mss.tools.to_png(sct_img.rgb, sct_img.size, output)` which creates the whole PNG structure. If we omit the `output` argument, nothing is written to the file but instead the PNG raw bytes are returned (new in version 3.1.2). But it makes no differences.

I benchmarked on my OS (I am using SSD):

    # Just grabbing
    926

    # Grabbing + accessing `sct_img.rgb`
    660

    # Grabbing + to_png(output=None)
    251

    # Grabbing + to_png(output=output)
    249

As you can see, there are 2 rooms of improvement: [Screenshot.rgb](https://github.com/BoboTiG/python-mss/blob/fe69e5d8d99adce453422cb122c4ca848fd1c76e/mss/screenshot.py#L138-L140) and [to_png()](https://github.com/BoboTiG/python-mss/blob/fe69e5d8d99adce453422cb122c4ca848fd1c76e/mss/tools.py#L11). > Furthermore, this goes against the expected behavior of a config file: setting the same key twice should overwrite. This is why the list syntax exists.

I don't disagree with that statement, but I want to chime in that then at least newlines inside the list syntax should be permitted. The way it is right now, it's hard to provide nicely formatted configuration files for users, since parameters with `action="append"` all **must** be specified on a single line. That leads to things like this:

```
# The following MUST be on one line:
pbuilder-opts=[--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg, --debootstrapopts --keyring=/etc/apt/trusted.gpg, --mirror http://fileserver.maurusnet.test/debian]
```

It would be much nicer if we could do aligned config values:

```
pbuilder-opts=[--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg, 
               --debootstrapopts --keyring=/etc/apt/trusted.gpg, 
               --mirror http://fileserver.maurusnet.test/debian]
```

Using quotes inside the list also leads to unexpected behavior, since 

```
pbuilder-opts=["--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg", ...]
```

will lead to `ConfigArgParser.parse_args().pbuilder_opts = ['"--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg"']` whereas

```python
import configargparse
parser = configargparse.ArgParser()
parser.add_argument("--pbuilder_opts", dest="pbuilder_opts", action="append")
print(parser.parse_args())
```

when invoked with `--pbuilder-opts="--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg"` will result in `ConfigArgParser.parse_args().pbuilder_opts = ['--keyring /etc/apt/trusted.gpg']`, because Python's shell processing for `sys.argv` will unquote the argument value.

So I think that there is some middle ground to improve the config file parsing. The handling of quotes in lists might actually be considered a bug, right?

```
# modify model, here swapping softmax for linear activation
model.layers[-1].activation = activations.linear

# maybe remove the temp file
```

It's seem that `sphinx-quickstart` is not designed with extending in mind.

This code from `hieroglyph.quickstart` module which works with older Sphinx:

```
sphinx.quickstart.QUICKSTART_CONF += u"""

# -- Hieroglyph Slide Configuration ------------

extensions += [
    'hieroglyph',
]
"""
```

could be replaced with extending `d` dict in `ask_user` to work with new Sphinx:

```
def ask_user(d):
    """Wrap sphinx.quickstart.ask_user, and add additional questions."""

    ... few lines skipped ...

    # set a few defaults that we don't usually care about for Hieroglyph
    d.update({
        'extensions': ['hieroglyph'],
        ... few lines skipped ...
        })

	... few lines skipped ...
```

Which is possible because template file `sphinx/templates/quickstart/conf.py_t` contains list of extensions in this way:

```
# Add any Sphinx extension module names here, as strings. They can be           
# extensions coming with Sphinx (named 'sphinx.ext.*') or your custom           
# ones.                                                                         
extensions = [{{ extensions }}]
```

But it's not possible for other additions/tweaks (such as adding list of hieroglyph options into conf.py), because the template doesn't expect this use case.

So maybe you can try proposing few changes in Sphinx to make work on `hieroglyph-quickstart` easier. I have no idea if upstream would welcome this idea, but it's an option to try.

Looking at the code, it seems that these promises are synchronous by default.  This was a surprise to me too having come straight from javascript promises.  However, a little more digging shows that it's already considered by the developers.

Here's a small change to the code in the original post that uses AsyncIO.

```
from promise import Promise, set_default_scheduler
from promise.schedulers.asyncio import AsyncioScheduler
import urllib.request

url = 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/photos'

def success(data):
    print("promise success!")
    # print(data)

def reject(data):
    print("rejected!")
    print(data)

def getData(url):
    def func(resolve, reject):

        req = urllib.request.urlopen(url)
        if req.getcode() == 200:
            resolve(req.read())
        else:
            reject(Exception(req.getcode()))

    return Promise(func)


set_default_scheduler(AsyncioScheduler())

print("getting resource")
p = getData(url)
p2 = p.then(success, reject)
print("Waiting on promise")
Promise.wait(p2)
```

Alternatively, you can ```pip install gevent``` and then use
```
from promise.schedulers.gevent import GeventScheduler
...
set_default_scheduler(GeventScheduler())
...
```

There's also a ThreadScheduler in this package but, for me, that seems to give the same results observed in the original post.  Maybe there's something going on with the GIL.  Anyhows, event-loops make me happier.

Yeah, some cases (like in Python 2.7) `Threads` might still be necessary.

In this case, there will be an automatic `get_default_scheduler` that will return the best scheduler for the environment:
* Python 2.7: `Thread` (+`ThreadPoolExecutor ` ?) scheduler
* Python 3.4+: `AsyncIO` scheduler (using the event loop)

Also, there will be a setter `set_default_scheduler`, that will permit developer to use custom ones (probably also provided by this library):
* `Gevent` scheduler
* `Tornado` scheduler
* ...

```python
def spkezr(targ, et, ref, abcorr, obs):
    targ = stypes.stringToCharP(targ)
    ref = stypes.stringToCharP(ref)
    abcorr = stypes.stringToCharP(abcorr)
    obs = stypes.stringToCharP(obs)
    starg = stypes.emptyDoubleVector(6)
    lt = ctypes.c_double()
    if not hasattr(et, "__iter__"):
        et = [et]
    state = []
    times = []
    for t in et:
        libspice.spkezr_c(targ, ctypes.c_double(t), ref, abcorr, obs, starg, ctypes.byref(lt))
        checkForSpiceError(None)
        state.append(stypes.vectorToList(starg))
        times.append(lt.value)
    return numpy.squeeze(state), numpy.squeeze(times)

There are three basic cases I thought about:
1. `#_ value` → raise an EOF error
2. `#_ value1 value2` → parse as `value2`
3. `value1 #_ value2` → parse as `value1`

root_expression = expression
                | discarded_expression
                | discarded_expressions expression
                | expression discarded_expressions
```

Hello Loreto,

to be honest, I don't see much similarity between the two, except that 
they both use the term "encoder".

- Our BPE implementatoin performs word segmentation into subword units. 
It does not perform translation or transliteration, but it can be 
helpful to improve the performance of a translation system because some 
translations/transliterations are easier on the subword level.

- The Google FST you refer to is a full "translation" system (consisting 
of a translation model and an n-gram language model) with no reordering 
component. See here for some more work on using FSTs for MT: 
http://www.statmt.org/survey/Topic/FiniteStateMachines

best wishes,
Rico

@veox if I may ask, why the choice for urllib and http.client over requests? To stay more to standard python libs? Or is there another technical reason you saw? Maybe just personal preference, I personally would have used requests out of preference, though not a problem. Saw this issue browsing and thought it interesting is all.

* "5 bytes": #130
* terminal escapes: hm. I'm inclined to say that if you `wormhole rx` from jerks using a terminal that can't display arbitrary bytes safely, then you get what you paid for. But I have to admit that I want better than that. Accepting files involves user confirmation (because you should know what proposed filesystem change you're about to allow, and to set an expectation of the transfer time based upon how large the file is). Accepting text messages does not, because that feels like a drag on what should be a simple operation. Maybe we could scan the message for codepoints below 0x20 and ask for confirmation iff they exist? Or offer to save the `--text=` message into a file instead? (OTOH if the user does `less FILENAME` right away, do they get hit with the same attack?)

And if we do that, we should think about sneaky filenames that include escape sequences. I'd rather not have *two* confirmation questions, especially if the first is an "are you willing to be asked about a filename that has unusual characters?" metaquestion.

I guess the deeper question is how to enable predictable subprocess/pipe -based interaction with the `wormhole` executable, similar to how Git's distinction between "plumbing" and "porcelain". I'd expected the internal (python) library to be the "plumbing" API, but I'm gratified that we've moved beyond just-python already.

There are plenty of other tools/options/issues to make that work better (pipe-mode #85, `--write-code-to-fd=` #104, overwrite #73), but since the main purpose of the executable tool is to move files around for humans, I think I'm still looking for a way to require a UTF-8 -capable locale, and to allow the stderr status messages to emit UTF-8 if they need to (which is only when the humans used something non-ASCII, like a filename or `--text=` message).

in the case where left is included and right is excluded a position described as i:i would refer to the location between i-1 and i, not including either i-1 or i themselves.

to give context for this, a description of an insertion mutation in a genome would refer to a position between nucleotides as the location of origin for the variant, because that "range" doesn't exist in the reference sequence, but rather has a 'location' that lies between existing positions

> Is there a way to make the parser work with those characters from a utf-8 encoded file?

It already does. The problem is that you can't _print_ these directly, which is a Python 2 quirk.

Python internal `print` code has [a special case](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Objects/fileobject.c#L2567-L2574) for `unicode` objects that encodes them to `sys.stdout.encoding` (`'UTF-8'` on my system). In general, though, it just [calls the object's `__str__` method](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Objects/object.c#L303), which has no knowledge of what encoding to use.

In mwparserfromhell's case, `parsed` is a `Wikicode` object that stores its data as unicode, but is not unicode _itself_ (because then it would have to be immutable, but you're allowed to change `Wikicode` objects). Its [`__str__` method](https://github.com/earwig/mwparserfromhell/blob/develop/mwparserfromhell/string_mixin.py#L60) is the same as calling `__str__` on the underlying unicode object. `print parsed` effectively becomes `print unicode(parsed).encode('ascii')`, which won't work when it contains non-ASCII characters.

The best thing if you really want to print it is to take advantage of the special-casing for unicode mentioned above and do the type conversion explicitly: `print unicode(parsed)`. Any other operations on the wikicode should be fine by default.

```
functions:

    handler: wsgi.handler
    events:
      - http: ANY {proxy+}
```

First of all, note that ``st-table`` is simply a way to name and expose a variable (which represents the displayed data set ie sorted,filtered,sliced) to the template and to the parent controller, if required. But the var does not have to exists in your parent controller if you don't need it (it will simply be created on the current scope by smart-table).

1) however if you base your data set to an existing collection in you parent controller, when smart-table creates its own controller a copy of this data set is created and any further table logical operation (slice, sort, search) will be based on this initial data set. Any change in the table state will trigger a set of operations to perform on this initial data set and will create a new version of the displayed collection which the view is bound to.
If you use st-safe-src attribute you simply tells smart-table that you expect external changes to the initial data set and to watch for these changes. So smart-table will simply update its reference copy whenever an external change happen

so to sum up : 
always: smart-table creates a safe reference copy based on data passed at initial time
with st-safe-src: this copy is updated whenever an external change happens to it

2) this is a bit different use case. The role of smart table here is more to sync the different directives based on table state and forward to a server any change in table state, but it won't perform any logical operation as everything will happen on the server. In this particular case you expect that the data set sent by the server to EXACTLY match the table state, and therefore to be displayed as it is. That is why there is no need to hold a safe reference copy.

3) Yes I have actually a small prototype, I might share when I am back from holiday (mid-may). Obviously I have not yet a great knowledge of angular 2 internal and as the documentation is lacking at the moment, there will obviously have some changes to do. But the idea is to abstract data source, data flow through the chain of operations and this can actually be framework agnostic. The rest is component plumbing, component state sync, etc

The problem here is that it's impossible (as far as I know) to detect the width of the PyCharm console. The fallback result is that the progressbar defaults to the "standard" console width of 79 characters.

You can work around it by manually specifying the terminal width:
```python
bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(
    widgets=widgets,
    term_width=120,
)
```

When using snapshot testing with Jest, we use Rosie.js to generate test data and also reset sequences during setup.

Dynamic data outside of our hands, like any datetime field with auto_now_add, we just mock:

```
@mock.patch('django.utils.timezone.now', lambda: datetime.datetime(2017, 10, 17))
def test_anything(self):
    ...
```

This is because "san" is currently in the prefixes constant. Prefixes join to the piece after it. This is because there are a few Spanish last names that use "san" as a prefix:

http://genealogy.familyeducation.com/browse/origin/spanish?page=7

* San Jose
* San Juan
* San Martin
* San Miguel
* San Nicolas
* San Pedro
* San Roman


You can fix this in your own project by removing 'san' from the prefixes constant:

```python
    >>> from nameparser.config import CONSTANTS
    >>> CONSTANTS.prefixes.remove('san')
```

I guess the parser could only join prefixes for last names, which seems reasonable.

I've investigated the issue. The real problem is that `jdj_tags.extensions.DjangoI18n` has the same priority attribute (100 by default from superclass) with basic `jinja2.ext.InternationalizationExtension` and serve same tags (jinja2 extension - `tags = set(['trans'])`, jdj extension - `tags = set(['trans', 'blocktrans'])`). So the order, they are iterated in `jinja2.environment.Evironment.iter_extensions` is not obvious. And this method is used by `jinja2.parser.Parser`, when it gets extensions for parsing tags and froms `self.extensions` dictionary. So standard library sometimes replaces jdj library in dictionary, sometimes - not. You should set priority > 100 to you extensions which have tags intersecting with standard jinja2 tags.

Boost.Python is for building Python C extensions. You can take a look at the gameboycore-python source code to see how I'm doing it.

You create user-config.jam in the boost root directory. 

The Boost root directory is simply the path to the boost folder. So `BOOST_ROOT` would point to  `C:\boost_1_60_0` for example.

You can use the `SET` command if you want. For things like Boost I add an environment variable using Control Panel -> System -> Advanced System Settings.

Achieves the same thing, except its persistent. 

`PYTHON_ROOT` would point to your python installation. So for you I'm guessing its `C:\Python36`. But you'll have to check.

You can also add that through Control Panel as well. Or just use `SET` to make it temporary.

Per the first point, the general convention is to store addition claims in the `user_claims` key via the `user_claims_loader` decorator (http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/add_custom_data_claims.html). That said, this is just a convention, it is not enforced. You can absolutely store whatever you want in the identity/sub claim. 

You can still use the `claims_verification_loader` for data stored in the subs claim, you will just need to get it yourself (with `get_jwt_identity()` or `get_raw_jwt()`) instead of having it passed in via the function args. The reason the function takes the `user_claims` data as an argument is just to make it easier to use and clearer on what it does.

Per the second case, being able to change the name of the `user_claims` seems totally reasonable to me. If you wanted to submit a pull request with that change I would be happy to help get it merged. I can also look at adding it myself when I have some free time on my hands.

Hopefully this helps. Let me know if you have any follow up questions.

As @jgbishop pointed out, the faulkner patch only fixes the direct requests and the AJAX requests in the panel no longer work.  Here's a patch that worked for me:

replace: `if hasattr(panel, 'generate_stats'):`
with: `if hasattr(panel, 'generate_stats') and not panel.get_stats():`

Essentially, only generate the stats if they haven't been generated already.  This seems to prevent the double escaping and probably is more efficient, too.

Since this project seems to be dead, you can use this patch by inheriting and overriding the one method that has the issue and then using it in `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` instead.  Here's a patched middleware to use:

```
"""
This is to patch django-debug-panel to work with latest versions of 
django-debug-toolbar.  The issue is SQL is double-escaped so this
version only calls `generate_stats` on panels if they haven't
already been called.
"""
import threading
import time

import debug_panel
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse
from debug_panel.cache import cache
from debug_panel.middleware import DebugPanelMiddleware


class DebugPanelMiddlewareFixed(DebugPanelMiddleware):
    def process_response(self, request, response):
        """
        Store the DebugToolbarMiddleware rendered toolbar into a cache store.

        The data stored in the cache are then reachable from an URL that is appened
        to the HTTP response header under the 'X-debug-data-url' key.
        """
        toolbar = self.__class__.debug_toolbars.get(threading.current_thread().ident, None)

        response = super(DebugPanelMiddleware, self).process_response(request, response)

        if toolbar:
            # for django-debug-toolbar >= 1.4
            for panel in reversed(toolbar.enabled_panels):
                if hasattr(panel, 'generate_stats') and not panel.get_stats():  # PATCH HERE
                    panel.generate_stats(request, response)

            cache_key = "%f" % time.time()
            cache.set(cache_key, toolbar.render_toolbar())

            response['X-debug-data-url'] = request.build_absolute_uri(
                reverse('debug_data', urlconf=debug_panel.urls, kwargs={'cache_key': cache_key}))

        return response

```

1. While I agree that the documentation of this method should be better, the 3rd example actually shows an interesting feature of this method:

There is no difference in the result between the input `y= [[1.], [0.], [1.]]` and `y= [[1.], [0.], [2.]]`, because of a clipping applied to `y`. Since dimensions of `x` are `(3, 2)`, meaning that the last possible index of `axis=1` is `1`, the clipping applied to that last element of `y` will change `[2.]` to `[1.]`. That's exactly what the documentation means saying:

> By default, if any index mentioned is too large, it is replaced by the index that addresses the last element along an axis (the clip mode).

2. I agree, that the last example doesn't show the dimensionality quite well. But consider the code below:

```
import mxnet as mx
x = mx.nd.array([
    [[ 1.,  2.], 
     [ 3.,  4.], 
     [ 5.,  6.]],
    [[ 11.,  12.], 
     [ 13.,  14.], 
     [ 15.,  16.]]
])
y = mx.nd.array([[ 1., 1.], [ 0., 0.], [ 1., 0.]])

x.shape # will print: (2, 3, 2)
y.shape  # will print (3, 2)

mx.nd.pick(x, y, axis=0)
```

The output will be:
```
[[ 11.  12.]
 [  3.   4.]
 [ 15.   6.]]
<NDArray 3x2 @cpu(0)>
```
In this case we use `y` which is one dimension less than `x`. I think that was the main point of the documentation.

I think you are confused by the `act_data` argument in the `Activation` example. This is a key/value argument that is used to bind the symbol with the data. Because in the example the Activation symbol is named `act`, the default input symbol should be named `act_data`

For example this would work too:

```
    op = Activation(data=mx.sym.var('data0'), act_type=act_type, name='act')
    y = test_utils.simple_forward(op, data0=x)
```

@fgrcon sorry for not responding earlier. I actually had to take a look into this PLIP even though I didn't volunteer to review it. I got an error with a missing ``thumbSize`` value while upgrading a project.

While thumb is better than icon (thanks for changing that) I noticed that ``thumbSize`` is just wrong in terms of the semantics of Plone's image handling. A size is a dimension like 200:400 while a scale is a name for that size. You were using scale names but named the variable "size". That's nitpicking, but it's in core so it should be named consistently to avoid confusion. Also all ``ov_`` prefixes are not necessary. Why would one assume an attribute be called ``ov_thumbSize_summary`` while on ISiteSettings it's called ``thumb_size_summary``.
I would have given that as feedback if I would have commited to review your PLIP but I didn't because of time issues.

`2to3` has differnt transformations which you can all run separately.

I would add a config setting where you can add a list of transformations.
The checker should then run the fixes on by one (`2to3 -w -f except -- <ADDON BASE PATH>`) and check if anything has changed (`git diff --quiet -- <ADDON BASE PATH>`). After each check reset the changes (`git checkout HEAD -- <ADDON BASE PATH>`) so you can run git diff again.
The usage of git for checking if anything has changed is just a suggestion and you can do it in any other way if you want. 

The following transformations should can be applied for all Kodi/XBMC versions: dict
except, filter, has_key, import, itertools, map, ne, next, print, renames, types, xrange and zip
set_literal can be only run for Krypton (v17) and higher as it is only python 2.7 (and not 2.6) compatible.

Cool. So exceptions are marked in two places in the ``service-2.json`` models:

* Under the ``"errors"`` key for the operation definition. Here is an example for [ACM](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/acm/2015-12-08/service-2.json#L21). I could see this being useful for documentation purposes of what operations can throw what exceptions.
* Looking at the shapes, if a shape has the key ``"exception: true"`` than that is a possible exception that can be thrown. Here is an example in [ACM](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/acm/2015-12-08/service-2.json#L427). I could see this useful in enumeration and warning if you are importing an exception that does not exist.

Another important thing to note is that some services do not define exceptions, like EC2, so you would not be able to use the model for some of these services to enumerate/document exceptions. But in general, most services will have exceptions defined, especially the newer services.

Originally, that was the cleanest way to port of all of the existing functionality from boto3 over to the s3transfer method. I think the biggest blocker from making the architecture a single IO queue and single IO thread was that we use the IO queue as a two way for communication.

Take for example, [here](https://github.com/kyleknap/s3transfer/commit/8561dc7506700e50e78cdad65d8641ecb06884c1#diff-a12a8d2ff65118bd0968bf807b8cf8cdR268) where if we encounter an exception we signal back to the producer of the queue that we encountered a problem in writing so stop sending us more data. We cannot do this anymore if share a single queue for all downloads. Instead we would have to rely on the transfer coordinator or something.

I will look to see how far I can get in making it a single queue/thread strategy and get back to you. I have a feeling that I will have to rewrite a fair amount of the code that I used from boto3, but should be doable. I am concerned as well with a possibly large amount of threads each with a queue that could have a lot of tasks in the queue.

```ini
mako.directories = myapp:templates/
mako.precompile =
    myapp:templates/
    myapp:some_other_template.mako
```

When looking Chrome and Firefox supported shortcuts, the only used keys, when performing a click, are `Ctrl`, `Shift` and `Alt`. So I think we can go with that. Then the keyword syntax would look like:
```
| Click Element | locator | alt=False | ctrl=False | shift=False |
```
The order of arguments is not ideal in the example, because `alt` is least used. Perhaps `ctrl` or `shift` as first ones would be better.

The plan is to combine the keywords in such manner that `Open Browser` keyword allows more flexibility for the driver creation. You have to call StopClient() before you call StartClient() again, but yes, you can change the server address in ntcore at runtime.  If it wasn't possible before, that was an implementation (or API) constraint, not a protocol constraint.

As ntcore runs on both the robot, DS, and coprocessors, and possibly multiple instances of it might be running simultaneously on the DS and/or coprocessor (e.g. SmartDashboard plus GRIP plus a user program), the approach of getting the IP address would need to change before I would consider integrating it.  In future years, we could theoretically solve the narrow problem of multiple DS-local apps by doing a doorbell-type server in the DS app and connecting to localhost (e.g. reversing the current dashboard TCP connection), but that doesn't solve the coprocessor problem.  If the robot IP address is not fixed, some discovery method is required.  While mDNS has issues, adding a doorbell protocol to the DS app doesn't seem like the best solution, as after all the DS still needs to find the robot!  If we had more consistent DNS that would likely be the best possible method.  I think the main thing I'd need to add to ntcore in that case would be to take a list of client addresses to iterate over rather than just one, so it would e.g. try "roborio-xx.lan", then "roborio-xx.local", etc.

This is quite a nasty issue for Windows users. It's really easy to run into it, and when you run into it the only solution is to either recreate the virtualenv (not possible if you do this in the main interpreter) or to poke through the virtualenv and manually delete `pywin32`'s `dll`-s and `pyd`-s before trying again.

The issue happens when you have one version of `pywin32` installed and you wish to upgrade it, or instead of upgrading you install a package that requires a different `pywin32` version.

For most of the people it's probably not an issue to delete and recreate the venv, but for some (like my use case) who have a deploy pipeline where large virtualenvs are upgraded in place with new package version, this issue is a show stopper.

So to give an example, let's say you have pywin32 already installed:
```
D:\temp\test_venv>v27\Scripts\python.exe -m pip install pypiwin32==219
Looking in indexes: [...]
Collecting pypiwin32==219
  Downloading https://pypi.[...]/root/wheelhouse/+f/561/8522ad9c2b229/pypiwin32-219-cp27-none-win32.whl (
6.7MB)
    100% |UUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUU| 6.7MB 3.9MB/s
Installing collected packages: pypiwin32
Successfully installed pypiwin32-219
```

and then you wish to upgrade it, or you wish to install/upgrade a package which requires a different pywin32 version:
```
D:\temp\test_venv>v27\Scripts\python.exe -v -m pip install -vvv pywin32==223
[...]
Installing collected packages: pywin32

Could not install packages due to an EnvironmentError.
Consider using the `--user` option or check the permissions.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:\temp\test_venv\v27\lib\site-packages\pip\_internal\commands\install.py", line 347, in run

It's unfortunate that there are many places that during a `pip install` could load the `pywin32` dlls.

It used to be in `pip`, but they patched their `appdirs` module to not do so.

But unfortunately `pip` imports `setuptools` whose `appdirs` module (inside `pkg_resources`) does load the `pywin32` dlls. So you're stuck.

Another example of code that loads `pywin32` dlls during `pip install` is the `platform` module from Python's stdlib. A `setup.py` I had used the `platform` module, but I managed to remove the usage.

pykafka requires `byte` strings for specifying consumer group and topic, so you want to change:

```py
        client = KafkaClient(hosts="10.10.10.11:9092")
        topic = client.topics["test"]
        self.balanced_consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
            consumer_group="test_group",
            auto_commit_enable=True,
            zookeeper_connect="storm_1:2181,storm_2:2181,storm_3:2181"
        )
```

to 

```py
        client = KafkaClient(hosts="10.10.10.11:9092")
        topic = client.topics[b"test"]
        self.balanced_consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
            consumer_group=b"test_group",
            auto_commit_enable=True,
            zookeeper_connect="storm_1:2181,storm_2:2181,storm_3:2181"
        )
```

from photutils.psf import IntegratedGaussianPRF, DAOGroup
from astropy.table import Table
from photutils.psf import BasicPSFPhotometry


fname = '/Users/covino/ownCloud/Data/GW/VST/G211117/2015-12-27/G211117_VST_r_20151227_p0.fits'
data = getdata(fname)

X = 16413.1409838
Y = 10859.3948134

i = np.rint(X).astype(np.int)-1
l = np.rint(Y).astype(np.int)-1
size = 75
image = data[l-size:l+size,i-size:i+size]

mmm_bkg = MMMBackground()
fitter = LevMarLSQFitter()

FWHM = 5.0
sigma_psf = FWHM/gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm
daogroup = DAOGroup(3.0*sigma_psf*gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm)

psf_model = IntegratedGaussianPRF(sigma=sigma_psf)
psf_model.x_0.fixed = True
psf_model.y_0.fixed = True

pos = Table(names=['x_0', 'y_0'], data=[[X-np.rint(X)+size],[Y-np.rint(Y)+size]])

photometry = BasicPSFPhotometry(group_maker=daogroup,bkg_estimator=mmm_bkg,psf_model=psf_model,fitter=LevMarLSQFitter(),fitshape=15,aperture_radius=5.)
rt = photometry(image=image, positions=pos)
print("Aperture 5 pixels: ", rt['flux_fit'][0])


photometry = BasicPSFPhotometry(group_maker=daogroup,bkg_estimator=mmm_bkg,psf_model=psf_model,fitter=LevMarLSQFitter(),fitshape=15,aperture_radius=7.)

I've seen this before. Even if the package was installed without `sudo`, if you use `sudo` to try to compile it the compiled and generated files - one of which is `skimage/version.py` - will have elevated privileges. 

The solution is to run 
``` bash
sudo chown -R your_username_here ./*
```
in your skimage root directory. That should transfer all files to your user privilege level. Then run `python setup.py develop` again.

The wheels need to be built on something even earlier, which is the Manylinux standard, based on a well-past-end-of-life Centos 5 distribution.  This is deliberate, to make sure that the wheels only use versions that almost any Intel Linux will have.   In the scikit-image-wheels repo, we build on Manylinux (which we have to do, to make the standard wheels) and then test on Trusty.   We could test on something else, but as far as I know we haven't yet hit a problem on something other than Trusty, when the Trusty tests pass.

If i put `user_info` in `def includeme`, the views are not registered. 

I like the pyramid *imperative* style of defining views and grouping all needed information in one place `def includeme` (separating views and configuration).

Mixing *declarative*  and *imperative* with `@view_config` and `add_route` and in worst case seperating them in two files, makes it for me to cluttered.

Anyway i got it working by calling `add_cornice_service` see `XXX`:

```python
from collections import defaultdict
from cornice import Service

_USERS = defaultdict(dict)

def get_info(request):
    username = request.matchdict['username']
    return _USERS[username]

def set_info(request):
    username = request.matchdict['username']
    _USERS[username] = request.json_body
    return {'success': True}

def who(request):
    return {"usernames": _USERS.keys()}

def includeme(config):
    user_info = Service(name='users', path='/{username}/info')
    user_info.add_view('post', set_info)
    user_info.add_view('get', get_info)
    # XXX calling add_cornice_service to register views
    config.add_cornice_service(user_info)

    config.add_route("who", "/who")
    config.add_view(who, route_name="who", renderer="json")
```

But now the sphinx documentation is not being extracted. After modifying the sphinx extension, calling `includeme` i also got the sphinx docu working:

```python
class ServiceDirective(Directive):
....
    def run(self):
    ...
        import cornice
        from pyramid.config import Configurator
        config = Configurator()
        cornice.includeme(config)

        # import the modules, which will populate the SERVICES variable.
        for module in self.options.get('modules'):
            if module in MODULES:
                reload(MODULES[module])
            else:
                mod = import_module(module)
                if hasattr(mod, 'includeme'):
                    mod.includeme(config)
                MODULES[module] = mod
```

As i showed it is possible to register views with `add_view` in a `includeme`. As it seems it is only now the sphinx extension, documentation which should be edited maybe with an extra option `app_includeme`:

```
.. cornice-autodoc::
   :modules: your.module
   :services: name1, name2
   :service: name1 # no need to specify both services and service.
   :ignore: a comma separated list of services names to ignore
   :app_includeme: your.app
```

## Clarification for the Unicode point

Google faced the same issue with Gmail (see [blockpost](http://googleonlinesecurity.blogspot.ch/2014/08/protecting-gmail-in-global-world.html)) and decided to use a very good official specification called [Unicode Restriction Level](http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#Restriction_Level_Detection). We should choose one of the levels described there. I propose [3 - Highly Restrictve](http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#highly_restrictive).

For Python there exist a library that can be used to test this: [idncheck](https://github.com/soulshake/idncheck) (originally intended to check domain names, but the functionality is exactly what we need here)

## Blacklisted words
Should we blacklist some words (make it an admin option), e.g.
 - **system-like roles**: `guest`, `reviewer`, ...
 - **bad words** (maybe from a some anti-spam list)

## Length
Should we require a minimum length, e.g. 4 characters?

> But as shared above, my uWSGI config has `threads = 5`. So perhaps this is something else?

fwiw `enable-threads` is different from `threads`. `threads` is how many uWSGI threads to run to process requests, but `enable-threads` is to enable the use of the GIL and allow application layer threads in Python. This is important from raven-python since we'd prefer to use a thread to send our messages and not block the running application.

I'd agree with Feike that having an explicit way to distinguish between the encrypted and plain-text values is better than introducing some "magical" tokens, but I do't like the idea of converting the single-value keys like the username into the lists.

What if we change the keys themselves, and instead of doing

    username:
    password:
specify

    encrypted-username:
    encrypted-password:

for the encrypted values (the same way as PostgreSQL accepts ENCRYPTED PASSWORD when specifying a password for the user). That would keep the schema compatible with the current versions of Patroni, since the plain text values will still go into the "username" and "password" keys. In addition, it would also allow us to encrypt other values, not just usernames and passwords.

Well, it seems that the context parameter for *urlopen* exists in [2.7.9 <= Python < 3](https://docs.python.org/2/library/urllib2.html#urllib2.urlopen) and was reintroduced in [3.4.3](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/urllib.request.html). Thus, the test builds for Python 3.0 - 3.3 are broken. This issue was also discussed on [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36600583/python-3-urllib-ignore-ssl-certificate-verification) but the only answer was to upgrade Python. 

So, either merge this patch to the master branch and applications using Overpy with a Python3 version of < 3.4.3 will break, create a new branch which only support Python > 3.3 or simply neglect that pull request.

We have a One-To-Many relation and I want to have everything in a single object. We have this class Benefits linked to another class named Vendors.

`class Benefits(models.Model):
    id_vendor = models.ForeignKey(
        'Vendors', models.DO_NOTHING, related_name='benefits', db_column='id_vendor')
` 
When we update a Benefit object in its admin, we can update the associated Vendors object with this: 

`    def response_add(self, request, obj: Benefits, post_url_continue=None):
        # Ensure we update Algolia's index once all many-to-many fields have been updated.
        algoliasearch_django.save_record(obj.id_vendor)

        return super().response_add(request=request, obj=obj, post_url_continue=post_url_continue)

    def response_change(self, request, obj: Benefits):
        # Ensure we update Algolia's index once all many-to-many fields have been updated.
        algoliasearch_django.save_record(obj.id_vendor)

        return super().response_change(request=request, obj=obj)
`

> Also, Is there a way to dump the csv into one file without listing all of the messages?

No, not easily. Different messages are typically recorded at different rates with different timestamps. Merging them would generally require a resampling, which is why multiple files are created in the first place.
The sdlog2 was just less accurate in terms of timestamps, which is why it could create a single file. We could do the same here, but I think we should do better than that.

Hello @skprasadu 

`mark_event` provides an optional `now` argument which you can use to import data from your source. To speed up the process, you can also use Redis pipelines. With pipelines you provide a pipeline object as a system argument, and set `use_pipeline=False` as an argument value (yes, `use_pipeline=False` is not a typo, but rather a horribly misleading name for the variable).

Here is a trivial example

```python
import datetime
import bitmapist

r = bitmapist.get_redis()
pipe = r.pipeline()
uid = 1
for day in range(365):
    dt = datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1) + datetime.timedelta(day)
    bitmapist.mark_event('foo', uid, now=dt, system=pipe, use_pipeline=False)

pipe.execute()
```

Hi Helen,

There are a few reasons we don't currently want to merge this into our main GitHub repository.

- This pull request gives the impression that RHEL 5 is supported with a standard package install. The configuration needed to make this work on a vanilla RHEL 5 image is non-trivial because EL 5 uses Python 2.4 by default. There are also several features of the guest environment I do not believe would work properly using these changes.

- Changes in your pull request specific to RHEL 5 should not be used on other Linux distributions. In particular, the way the sudoers group is created is suboptimal on newer Linux distributions. Even on RHEL 5, I would suggest modifying the file differently (you can create a new file, write to it, and swap it into place).

We'll keep these changes in a separate GitHub repository so that we can continue to provide them to users who might find them helpful, but I'm closing off the pull request.

The built-in Column classes add themselves to a private list, and each Column has an attribute called ``handles_field_classes`` that is a list of the modelfields it can query for searches.  (BooleanColumn lists ``models.BooleanField`` and ``models.NullBooleanField`` for example, because you query those the same way in the database.)

Using the following example:

```python
class MyDatatable(Datatable):
    class Meta:
        model = BlogEntry
        columns = ['id', 'name', 'is_published']
```

At this stage, the column system doesn't know anything about ``is_published``, so it looks up the field on the model.  It discovers that it's a Django ``BooleanField``, but it still needs to look up a Column class to handle BooleanFields.  It iterates over the private column registry until it finds a Column class with a ``handles_field_classes`` list that contains the model field BooleanField.

Normally, it iterates the registry until it finds the built-in BooleanColumn, but if you declare a new column class, it goes into the top of the registry with top priority, and will be found first.

As a counter-example, this Datatable would respect your choice for BooleanColumn because you've provided it explicitly:

```python
class MyDatatable(Datatable):
    is_published = columns.BooleanColumn("Published")

    class Meta:
        model = BlogEntry
        columns = ['id', 'name', 'is_published']
```

Hopefully that clarifies it!

shadowsocks对发送的数据做包装和加密，每增加一跳发送的包都会额外加上一个包头。浪费带宽，尤其对于udp，因为每次udp请求都要单独包装。
用端口转发的方法可以实现多跳，直接用一个或多个中继在ssserver 和sslocal之间做转发。ssserver和local之间的通信本身就是加密的。中继只转发加密后的数据，不负责任何解析。安全性是一样的。

```
(not including the backtraces, although I have them if necessary.)
 
But the most relevant bit (I think), is that I was able to use https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Linux_Applications_Debugging_Techniques/Deadlocks to determine that a thread {{LWP 189893}} is supposed to be holding this mutex. And that thread is dead (ie, not in the threads list above).

Rust's locks are supposed to use "poisoning", meaning that it should be impossible for a thread holding a lock to die while holding it, because they will either 1) exit a block cleanly, allowing the lock's guard to be dropped, or 2) panic, which should poison the lock such that the next thread that attempts to acquire it also panics (avoiding a deadlock like this). Looking at the backtrace that the hung thread is in, it appears to be hung in a plain-jane Rust libstd `Mutex` ([here](https://github.com/Amanieu/thread_local-rs/blob/c3fe9ef7187f88e83055c4eb9a745e11e76e3f9c/src/thread_id.rs#L40)), meaning that the lock poisoning should definitely apply.

-----

So, in conclusion, my current hypothesis is that there is a combination of factors that means that we might currently re-spawn some work after calling [Scheduler.pre_fork](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/008ce42cb55e529319f53abeb00fc6cf3918f548/src/python/pants/engine/scheduler.py#L316-L317), but before forking. I'm going to spend some time today/tomorrow exploring this angle... and in particular, I'm going to consider picking #5521 back up.

@patricklaw, @baroquebobcat  : So, it looks like this ticket is relatively tightly coupled to ivy resolution as it exists today.

With the architecture of ivy as it stands, it's not safe to cache tasks that extend `IvyTaskMixin`, because it caches the raw report file. But even if it were to cache the translated/symlinked report file, it would not actually be able to ever use the cache entry, because the ivy report for the cache entry wouldn't exist.

The reason enabling caching for `cache.bootstrap.bootstrap-jvm-tools` doesn't work is _not_ because there is something unsafe about caching the shading, but rather because by extending IvyTaskMixin, it trips on the issue mentioned above.

Until Ivy resolution is rearchitected to cache reports (@baroquebobcat will begin this soon), I'd like to propose that we disable/remove caching for IvyTaskMixin, so that it uses the invalidation framework, but never tries to actually read/write cached artifacts (regardless of the settings on concrete tasks it is mixed into). This will allow us to safely enable caching for `cache.bootstrap.bootstrap-jvm-tools`.

Hi Felix, thanks for your feedback.

At least for the semantic graphs we cannot assume the length of the metadata matches the length of the input sentence. In this specific case, the metadata is a graph currently represented as an adjacency list. This means that, in theory, the "length" in terms of number of edges can vary between n and n^2, where n is the length of the sentence. I think this rules out the approach used by OpenNMT, unfortunately.

I also think extending the input tensor might not be enough. Specifically for the semantic graphs (but also for syntactic trees), the idea is that the structure provided by the metadata will guide operations in their respective encoder. For instance, a GCN encoder would use the graph information to perform convolutions on word embeddings according to their neighbours in the graph. This means that operations happen on upper layers beyond the Embedding one.

I have done the required changes, but I require your advice for code style:
In the metadata for a Sonos favorite, there is a `<resMD>` tag which contains the metadata of the item to which the favorite refers. To make its contents accessible to the user, there are two possible implementations:

1. When the DidlFavorite object is created, parse the resMD metadata and make the referenced object available via an attribute which can be accessed and modified. When the favorite it is converted back to XML, call `to_didl_string()` on the referenced object to get the metadata back.
This can be archieved by adding a resource_meta_data property - see https://github.com/ghcs27/SoCo/tree/favorite_data_structure.
2. Keep the resMD metadata in its own attribute. Make it accessible by adding a `reference` property which parses the metadata on read. For this, see https://github.com/ghcs27/SoCo/tree/favorite_data_structure_alt.

Which implementation do you prefer?

Cheers for pointing out that the `ipaddr` module supports this functionality too. I've removed the check requirements for the python3 module and merged this to provide the same functionality across both.

I've done some tests with Juniper, Mikrotik and Ubiquiti.

A Juniper configured as passive with the default TTL of 1 sends the SYN+ACK packet with a TTL of 255 and it switches to TTL 1 after it receives the first BGP OPEN message from the peer.

Mikrotik and Ubiquiti are more interesting. Both run Linux kernels. Whatever value I specify for the TTL, when configured as passive peers, both send the SYN+ACK packet with a TTL of 64 which seems to be hardcoded. After the three way handshake has been completed they switch to the configured TTL.

If I enable the MINTTL with a hack (as the IP_MINTTL constant is not defined in Python 2.7 I have put the value manually) ExaBGP can't complete the connection to Mikrotik or Ubiquiti (their SYN+ACK packet is discarded) but it should work with JunOS, I will try later today.

You can reduce the value further, all it does is reduce the idle sleep time, so the reactor will run more often but all it will check is if there was any new data on the filedescriptors with poll - so the CPU overhead should really be minimal.

> How should we test the python version in our code base

in general, checking `sys.version_info` is the right way. You can also import `PY2` and `PY3` constants from `fontTools.misc.py23` module, which come from sys.version_info anyway.

> interpolatable

This means that only master TTFs are built, but not the final variable font. The `ttf-interpolatable` format option was introduced before the `variable` one, and at that time the only way to make a variable font was to first make the master interpolatable TTFs, and then run fontTools.varLib.main script on them.

---

Bo, I actually don't think we need an extra folder for storing the output variable font.
It's never going to be more than one file anyway, what's the point in creating a folder then?

If you also find confusing that the generated *-VF.ttf ends up next in `master_ufo` folder, then we can just change that and save the variable font in the current folder.

BTW, I've always found fontmake's use of hard-coded destination folders ("master_ufo", "instance_ttf") not ideal. I wish these could be customized somehow. The `-o` option for specifying the output _format_ instead of the _destination_ is quite unusual, I believe. But these are probably different issues.

```python

If you copy the `Scripts\diamond` to `Scripts\diamond-cli.py` and run that, then it fixes this.

It's because the multiprocessing module tries to re-import the `Scripts\diamond` module and fails because it doesn't end in `.py`.

Renaming `Scripts\diamond` to `Scripts\diamond.py` breaks things because you can't import the rest of the diamond library.  Altogether, the way the multiprocessing stuff works on Windows is confusing and brittle.

javascript but i could not make it work, I'm maybe doing something wrong.
My approach is rather straightforward: search only for combinations that 
are "reasonable" (no crossings of 2345 fingers etc). This is coded in 
function Hand.skiprules(). This trims a lot of combinations to search 
through (if i remember it reduces to a few hundreds).
I didn't want to use a database because:
It's so boring... :)
It's not dynamic (the hand is elastic, it somehow remembers past 
positions, i gave it first try in set_fingers_positions(), but didn't 
have time to explore it deeper)
Doesn't allow for different hand sizes (which i think it's kind of 
important to consider)
Viterbi's algorithm could improve speed but at moment an average score

@nkeim @caspervdw @tacaswell I added another simple but very useful enhancement to this PR, revealing my motivation for this slicing work.

With a simple decorator, you can apply any image-processing function to a pims object and get lazy evaluation and slicing.

```
@pims.pipeline
def color_channel(image, channel):
    return image[channel, :, :]

red = color_channel(v, 0)
green = color_channel(v, 1)
```

Now `red` and `green` are `SliceableIterator` objects. They close over the function arguments, lazily processing the images on the way out.

Pipeline functions are also composable: `func1(func2(func3(v))))` does the right thing.

The "simple case" behavior of `color_channel` is unchanged. If you pass it a numpy array or a `pims.Frame`, the pipeline decorator does nothing.
Done, also added docstrings for the new names `is_rgb` and `is_interleaved`

Turns out that the `timeout` parameter of `websockets` does not work as expected!
citing from the documentation:
> The timeout parameter defines the maximum wait time in seconds for completing the closing handshake and, only on the client side, for terminating the TCP connection. close() will complete in at most 4 * timeout on the server side and 5 * timeout on the client side.

`websockets` does not provide any other mechanism to timeout connections.

The author suggests using `with asyncio.wait_for(connect(...), your_timeout)`. You can read it [here](https://github.com/aaugustin/websockets/issues/428#issuecomment-397858402).

I'm not exactly sure where we're disagreeing yet.  I feel like we might be talking past each other a little bit.  Did you see my comment buried in the "outdated" comments here: https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/6229#discussion_r147294379

It might help to step back and make sure we both have the same understanding of current behavior. I believe current behavior in the 4.3.x branch (not this PR) is as follows:

1. When CLI input is the only source of `channels` configuration information, then `defaults` is appended to the ordered set of channels.
2. When channel configuration is given in any configuration source other than CLI configuration, `defaults` is not added.
3. EXCEPT, when channels are over-specified.  That is, when, for each channel given in non-CLI configuration (either config files or environment variable config), the channel is also given in CLI configuration, then `defaults` is added.

Do you agree that is an accurate characterization of current behavior?

Here's an example, where I just print out the set of channels:

```
$ cat ~/.condarc
channels:
  - rdkit

$ python -m conda install blarg -c conda-forge -c bioconda
('conda-forge', 'bioconda', 'rdkit')  # not over-specified, no defaults

$ python -m conda install blarg -c conda-forge -c bioconda -c rdkit
('conda-forge', 'bioconda', 'rdkit', 'defaults')  # rdkit is over-specified, and 'defaults' is added

$ CONDA_CHANNELS=conda-forge python -m conda install blarg -c conda-forge -c bioconda -c rdkit
('conda-forge', 'bioconda', 'rdkit', 'defaults')  # conda-forge and rdkit both over-specified, and defaults is added

$ CONDA_CHANNELS=conda-forge python -m conda install blarg -c bioconda -c rdkit
('bioconda', 'rdkit', 'conda-forge')  # rdkit is over-specified, but conda-forge isn't, so no defaults added
```
I know for sure this is resolved in conda 4.4.  Hopefully we'll be able to focus on getting 4.4 through canary soon, now that Anaconda 5 is out.
