Whatever else may be going on, your Elasticsearch cluster is overloaded.  It should never take even 30 seconds to delete a batch of indices.  You may simply be suffering from master/client node communication timeouts (though I'm surprised there's not logs to indicate such) due to the overloaded nature of your cluster. 1250 indices, presuming the default 5+1 shard count, divided among 20 nodes is 625 shards per node, which is a rather high number.  By default, Elasticsearch allocates 10% of the heap it gets to index caching and management. Each active shard will want 250M of that 10%, and each inactive shard will want 4M of that 10%.  Even if you had zero active shards across your nodes, 625*4M = 2.5GB of that 10%.  That would suggest you would need 31G heaps on each node (10% would be 3.1G).  If you are not using 31G heaps, you are possibly putting a high amount of memory pressure on the index cache.  Elasticsearch will try to be smart about how it handles this memory.  A shard becomes inactive 30 minutes after the last indexing activity.  Elasticsearch can probably squeeze the memory down to about 47M per active shard (though indexing performance is degraded) and just under 1M for inactive shards.  When Elasticsearch can't squeeze that memory any more, it will suddenly, and without warning, stop being able to index new data.  The cluster will still be up, and search will still work.  You just won't be able to index any more.  This typically only happens in time-series index situations as shard count per node becomes an issue.

Also of concern, with regard to shard count per node, is that deleting indices will result in a massive cluster rebalance.  Elasticsearch, by default, will try to keep a similar number of shards per node.  If you delete a bunch in a big batch, it will try to rebalance, which could also result in severely degraded disk and network I/O.

You may simply have to try pruning your indices in much smaller batches and wait for the cluster to recover in between.

Our current policy is to treat exit codes as the success or failure of the command being executed.  This, as Troy mentioned, allows folks to have consistent expectations across all CLI commands.  

This also matches the industry pattern for POSIX where **any non-zero value** is consider an error.  And, of course, an `exists` function that did not find anything still worked as intended.  

I would recommend checking with BASH's conditional validation (better handling of param expansion, easier to debug with `set -ex`, and way safer than `test` / `[`):
```shell
if [[ -z $(az storage file exists -p ${path}) ]]; then
    echo "File exists"
else 
    echo "File does not exist"
fi
```

@annatisch last commit was debugged directly with @vishrutshah in peer programming. This allows to accept as key in the dict (serialisation) both the JSON key name and the Python key name.
Let's say RestAPI uses "awesomeName", so Python will use "awesome_name". Now for subtyping, both syntax are correct in a dict.
This allows to figure out subtyping even from a dict (serialisation) and from a JSON (deserialisation) even if the key are not expected to be the same. Also, it's been a long time I think about accepting both keys syntax in dict serialization (to simplify a copy/paste from a RestAPI example), so I'm shocked and don't consider this a hack.
Unittest to be added before merging this PR.

PyCon sounds exciting! Hope it was fun 👍 I missed the management packages -- I'll change the naming to match. I'll also fix the dependency in setup.py. On the point about workspace ID, coincidentally I have a PR out with [that change](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/3084/files#diff-5068384c2d0c7a349dffd27ac8f5ae2cL131) 😄 I also updated the readme there for generation which (of course) produced [another PR](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/2568). To the last bit about the string parameter: [this is the field](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/master/specification/operationalinsights/data-plane/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/stable/v1/OperationalInsights.json#L238) in the swagger. I saw models all have code like this: ```python _attribute_map = { 'name': {'key': 'name', 'type': 'str'}, 'columns': {'key': 'columns', 'type': '[Column]'}, 'rows': {'key': 'rows', 'type': '[[str]]'}, } ``` I had a chance to test this out and I see see that the python client does respect the string types in the swagger, converting the JSON type, e.g.: ``` [ u'OpsManager', u'2018-04-08T12:27:10.86Z', u'00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001', u'40.83.215.18', u'Direct Agent', u'Windows', u'', u'10', u'0', u'8.0.11081.0', u'Direct', 'False', '-121.83', '37.32', u'United States', u'microsoft.compute', u'virtualmachines', u'Azure', u'"security", "serviceMap", "securityCenterFree"', u'Heartbeat' ] ``` For context, here's the JSON returned from the HTTP call (note the boolean and raw number values returned, converted to strings): 
[
    "OpsManager",
    "2018-04-22T08:40:09.77Z",
    "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001",
    "40.83.215.18",
    "Direct Agent",
    "Windows",
    "",
    "10",
    "0",
    "8.0.11081.0",
    "Direct",
    false,
    -121.83,              # HERE 
    37.32,
    "United States",
    "microsoft.compute",
    "Azure",
    "\"security\", \"serviceMap\", \"securityCenterFree\"",
    "Heartbeat"
]
Answering my own question -- the client will respect whatever is in the swagger. My follow up, then: we have the [information for column types](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/2557/files#diff-d7af4bc32d13ab67d2652cc2204d2c6bR28). Could I do some custom modeling to convert/cast the underlying values so they look right from the service perspective? For example, a value returned as a number in JSON will come back as a string but I would convert it back using the column.type field to determine type. We are returning rows from DB query, but due to restriction in swagger/autorest we can't model the value as multiple primitive types (yet!). So we ended up with string. I'm trying to work around that at the SDK level to provide a better experience. Curious what you think.

> first I had to run pre-commit install --hook-type commit-msg in order to activate this, something I never had to to with other repositories.

`commit-msg` is a separate hook from `pre-commit` so it is installed separately (the same is also true for `pre-push` which is supported by `pre-commit`).  That is, they install different hook executables in `.git/hooks`.  All the other repositories you worked with were `commit` hooks and so the default `pre-commit install` set those up for you.

> Second problem is that I discovered that neither pre-commit run nor pre-commit run --all-files managed to run this check. They silently skip it.

`pre-commit run` takes a `--hook-stage` argument (which is usually used during invocation by specific hooks (`commit` / `commit-msg` / `push`).  To run a `commit-msg` hook you'd use `--hook-stage commit-msg`.

As for linting commits that already exist, you'd probably need to invoke `gitlint` in a different way (with `--commits`?) -- I haven't tried it myself but it looks like others https://github.com/jumanjihouse/autostager/commit/f50e61533ceb7d73abe00c2049a4e89f67baf38b#diff-82c3b36709926de3a1b27bea0d8258ccR50

For each class/pattern, train a HMM model.

Each component of the feature vector is 1D or 2D or 3D or etc. The dimension of the each component dependents on your feature extraction algorithms.

During predict, the unknown feature vector is fed in to each trained HMM. The maximum combined probability is the recognition result.

Here is an example to train HMMs for 8 gestures recognition:
```Python
from hmmlearn import hmm

def train_ghmm_foreachgesture(fvs):

    classcnt = 8
    nhs = 20
    model = []
    startprob = np.array([1.0/nhs for i in range(nhs)]) + 1
    # transmat = np.array([[0.7, 0.2, 0.1], [0.3, 0.5, 0.2], [0.3, 0.3, 0.4]])

    transmat = np.array([[1.0/nhs for i in range(nhs)] for j in range(nhs)]) + 1

    for i in range(classcnt):
        mi = hmm.GaussianHMM(n_components=nhs,
                            covariance_type="tied",
                            startprob_prior=startprob,
                            transmat_prior=transmat,
                            algorithm="viterbi",
                            n_iter=100,
                            params='stmc')
                            # params = "stmc")
        model.append(mi)

    for i in range(classcnt):
        fvi = fvs['gesture00{}'.format(i+1)]
        model[i].fit(np.array(fvi))

    return model

def predict(model, x):

    cmat = confusion_matrix(len(model))
    fvcnt = 0
    for i in range(len(model)):
        fvcnt += len(x['gesture00{}'.format(i+1)])

    dim = 63
    for f in range(len(x)):
        fv = x['gesture00{}'.format(f+1)]
        for v in range(int((len(fv)/dim))):
            vec = fv[v*dim:(v+1)*dim]
            p_max = np.finfo(np.float64).min
            label = -1
            for i in range(len(model)):
                p = model[i].score(np.array(vec))

                if p_max < p:
                    p_max = p
                    label = i
            if label == -1:
                aaaaaaaaaa=0
            cmat.feed(f, label)

    cmat.printcm()

    return

```

For me `aiohttp_sentry` isn't acceptable, it doesn't provide sufficient information and uses the much uglier "raven_client" approach. I wrote my own middleware precisely because I didn't want to use aiohttp_sentry.

Using logging is much cleaner because:
* you can capture errors from other services (eg. arq) with the raven config only defined once.
* can unit test error reporting by just changing the log handlers with no ugly mocking.
* As you said, you could send the data anywhere you like - to ELK, to papertrail, to sentry, to email via `SMTPHandler`, to foobar inc. application monitoring which doesn't even exist yet .... One implementation, all the integrations.

I understand (and completely agree with) keeping aiohttp lean, but this seems like a feature which:
1. a high proportion of people will want to use
2. it is important that people have the highest degree of confidence in it working - if your error reporting breaks "you're up **** creek with no paddle".

Up to you, I could release it as a seperate package if you'd prefer.

Sorry about that. Running `setup.py install` directly has not been recommended for some time, because you get either:

1. The default behaviour of setuptools, which is a major cause of headaches
2. The behaviour of plain distutils, which may not do everything you want.

We've mostly held out from requiring setuptools, so with our projects you'll mostly get 2. However, we generally now consider pip the correct way to install packages, so in a sense you're just running into the modern systems.

> With --no-deps, the dependencies are still recorded, giving errors at run-time instead of build-time.

Obviously if they are really hard dependencies, you're going to get errors at run time if they're not installed. But if you're getting entry point scripts which use pkg_resources and check for dependencies, I would *strongly* recommend upgrading to a newer version of pip. Modern versions no longer create those setuptools-style entry points.

Interesting idea, but I'm not convinced that we need to add anything to our process.

We already use milestones to indicate issues which we'd like to fix before a given release - e.g. [here's the 4.3 milestone](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/milestone/9). When preparing for a release, we go through those issues to either fix them or bump them to a later milestone. I've just done a pass on this for 4.3. In theory, we could save some time if we had an easy way to distinguish issues we consider important to fix from things that are less important, but we'd put the fix in that release if one was ready. But I suspect it wouldn't save that much time, and I think going back and looking at these issues again is probably healthy for the project.

If we do add a label, I'd argue that it should be the other way round, i.e. tag issues that we don't mind bumping to a later release. If we tag release blockers, we would still need to go through the issues without the tag to see if any of them should get the tag. I.e. if we add an issue to a milestone, I'd treat it as a release blocker by default.

The R kernel discussed this (or a subset of this ) problem recently: https://github.com/IRkernel/IRdisplay/issues/14 Basically: how should JS/css libs for visualisations be handled

The knitr/rmarkdown system in R has this problem solved: the "knit_asis" object (kind of like the display_data message) gets styling in an extra attribute [knit_meta](https://github.com/yihui/knitr/blob/23f1d6edcc675b2f93d4305387da213728146222/vignettes/knit_print.Rmd#metadata) which lists dependencies (each time such an object is produced) and rmarkdown (in this case in a similar role like the notebook frontend) manages what is outputted in the final document. Knitr/rmarkdown has is slightly easier, as it only makes one pass over the document and you can't reevaluate the notebook, so the solution here needs to handle reevaluating cells and removing styling when no cell references it (and this needs to be in the on-disc format as well...)

As such visualisations need to be available in html content which is derived from ipynb *files* (nbconvert, nbviewer, github,...), I don't think any "request from kernel" steps can be part of a solution for this problem, as the ipynb file needs to have all such dependencies available. A solutions should also handle that different output formats (html, latex,...) need different dependencies (message format and stored in the ipynb).

I've tested with QtConsole on py27 as well, and it was correct.  Python 2 has the *option* to store unicode as UCS-2 or UCS-4 (reflected in `sys.maxunicode`), chosen at build time (one of the many things fixed in Python 3). On Linux, I believe UCS-4 is default on 64b builds (~all users these days) and UCS-2 on 32b. It's UCS-2 by default on most darwin builds, for some reason. If it uses UCS-4, offsets will be correct, if it's UCS-2 they will have the same issue as js. So for the most part, Python frontends behave correctly, but javascript frontends have this bug.

Encoding this bug into released versions of the spec means that all of the correctly-behaving frontends (Python-based ones, such as QtConsole, jupyter-console, etc.) would retroactively get the inverse bug introduced, breaking code that was correct.

This is a case where it would have been really nice to include the frontend name and version in message headers, because changing the spec to backdate bugs introducing the same bug into packages that didn't have problems causes problems of its own.

Oh, I actually had to fix it in the source code. It comes from how `numpy` broadcasting works (it works its way back from the trailing dimension and for every trailing dimension they must match or either of the two must be one). So if you have two arrays `a` and `b` of shape `(1, 224, 224, 3)` and `(3, )` you can do `a-b`, but  if `a.shape = (1, 3, 224, 224)` the broadcasting for `a-b` fails.
Pytorch accepts tensors in the (BxCxHxW) format.

You can make you code work by setting 

```
preprocessing = (np.array([[[[104]], [[116]], [[123]]]]), 1)
```

and

```
adversarial = attack(np.transpose(image, (2, 0, 1)), label)
```

This gives you a preprocessing array of shape `(1, 3, 1, 1)` for which the broadcasting is valid and the correct input shape `(1, 3, 224, 224)` to the pytorch model.

I think this case should be automatically detected and solved inside the source code (as it is specific to the (NxCxHxW) case). I can make a pull request for this if you have a preferred way to deal with it

In the case of the OP's code, I think he means rewriting lines like this:

```python
B = np.zeros((4,4))
B[:2, :2] = A
```

as something like this:

```python
row1 = np.concatenate([A, np.zeros((2,2)),], axis=1)
row2 = np.zeros((2, 4))
B = np.concatenate([row1, row2], axis=0)
```

In general, instead of building arrays by allocating zeros and then assigning into blocks, you can instead build the blocks as separate arrays and then concatenate them. The latter method works with autograd, but the former method doesn't.

I don't think this is a pylast bug, rather you're submitting an incorrectly encoded artist name in the first example, in particular the unescaped `&` causes the problem and should be `%26`.

---

If you look at the JSON results from your first link, you'll see it gives an artist name like this:
```json
url: "https://www.last.fm/music/Major+Lazer+feat.+Justin+Bieber+/_/Cold+Water",
duration: "0",
...
listeners: "0",
playcount: "0",
...
artist: {
name: "Major Lazer feat. Justin Bieber ",
url: "https://www.last.fm/music/Major+Lazer+feat.+Justin+Bieber+"
},
```

---

If you URL encode (e.g. with http://www.url-encode-decode.com) "Major Lazer feat. Justin Bieber & MØ" you get `Major+Lazer+feat.+Justin+Bieber+%26+M%C3%98`. 

Sticking that in your first URL gives:

`http://ws.audioscrobbler.com/2.0/?method=track.getInfo&api_key=<insert_your_key>&artist=Major+Lazer+feat.+Justin+Bieber+%26+M%C3%98&track=Cold%20Water&format=json`

Resulting in the correct artist name:
```json
url: "https://www.last.fm/music/Major+Lazer+feat.+Justin+Bieber+&+M%C3%98/_/Cold+Water",
duration: "183000",
...
listeners: "1492",
playcount: "7014",
...
artist: {

* It doesn't fix a bug; it's an enhancement.

```python
def compute_gini(model):
    agent_wealths = [agent.wealth for agent in model.schedule.agents]
    x = sorted(agent_wealths)
    N = model.num_agents
    B = sum( xi * (N-i) for i,xi in enumerate(x) ) / (N*sum(x))
    return (1 + (1/N) - 2*B)
```

It's possible the two are related, but I think there are a few fundamental differences.  #253 was just calling methods of objects that weren't there or renamed.

The issue here is deeper: the way I think about it, `DalvikVMFormat` is a very direct translation of the DEX file.  It retains indices to objects, such as storing string indices for function names.  When you update the name, it very literally updates the name by updating the string in the pool.

A possible solution is to add another layer -- some object that represents all the classes in the DEX, containing just Classes, Fields, and Methods.  In this layer, the index data is removed.  If a method calls another method via invoke, the Instruction object doesn't contain the index and look it up using the ClassManager, the object just contains a reference to the other method.  

Another option: since the objects cache the string they refer to, set_name could update the string in `MethodId`/`FieldId`, but leave the string pool alone.  It would break consistency, the name index might refer to string "xyz" but the name is "abc".

```python
from itertools import islice
from ciscosparkapi import CiscoSparkAPI

api = CiscoSparkAPI()

messages = api.messages.list(roomId='<your-room-id>', max=10)

#get 11
for message in islice(messages, 11):
    print(message)
```

Which best describes what the ciscosparkapi package does:
1) Detects there are additional pages on the initial response, immediately goes through all available pages until all 1000 messages have been fetched.
2) Fetches the first page worth of messages only. When the loop reaches the 11th message it will then fetch the 2nd page.

https://github.com/CMA-ES/pycma/blob/881ad335a5c446a89752a279e47179472f9e7bb2/cma/transformations.py#L185
_Partly due to numerical considerations depend the values ``al`` and 
    ``au`` on ``abs(lb)`` and ``abs(ub)`` which makes the
    transformation non-translation invariant. In particular increases the
    interval In contrast to ``sin(.)``,
    the transformation is robust to "arbitrary" values for boundaries,
    e.g. a lower bound of ``-1e99`` or ``np.Inf`` or ``None``._

AFAICS, the reason for this apparent weird choice is possible numerical discretization errors (apart from the original conflicting goals to (i) ideally have the identity but also (ii) have a smooth function). I think this piece of code is the problem: if there is no message showing up for 100ms you'll exit the loop.
I suggest using some other means of knowing when to exit, e.g., message count, end of partition, KeyboardInterrupt, etc.
Also, since errors are signaled through poll() as messages with an error code set you need to make sure to check msg.error() and then dont treat that as a proper message but rather an error or informative (e.g., PARTITION_EOF) event.

@pganssle I was finally able to fix the history.

> I know you like using methods where functions might otherwise work. In most cases I cringe a little but recognize it doesn't really hurt anything. This is a case where I'll ask you to consider using functions where possible.

I don't know why you cringe at this, having a mix of instance methods and bare functions useful *only* for a given class breaks encapsulation. If it were a single function or a bunch of functions that were useful for other things, then I'd be more willing to use bare functions.

There is some case to be made that `Isoparser` is too big, and that it should be `IsoDateParser`, `IsoTimeParser` and `IsoTzParser`, with an `IsoParser` that contains one of each. I think there's basically no shared logic between the three.

The one method that I think may be useful to throw in `dateutil.utils` would be `Isoparser._calculate_weekdate`. The main reason I'm not doing that is that I'm not entirely sure what the right scope is for `dateutil.utils`. It's clearly a "miscellaneous bare functions" category, I'm wondering if it would be best broken down into smaller categories. I think a function that is the inverse of `date.isocalendar()` is useful outside of its utility in parsing ISO datetimes.

> There is eventually going to be a C/Cython version of this. Because perf is critical here, I'm guessing we'll end up going full-on C. Once there are two versions, we'll need to be super-careful to keep them in sync. Two things we can do to keep them in sync are 1) fine-grained unit tests to ensure their behaviors are the same and 2) keep the code structure parallel in each version. Using functions instead of methods makes this much easier, especially in the raw-C case.

In fact, I think one of the best reasons for encapsulation is that it makes it much easier to handle drop-in and partial replacement functions. Depending on exactly how we end up doing it, we can either have a `cdef` Cython class that calls either C or Cython `cdef` methods and is a drop-in replacement for `Isoparser`, or possibly we can have a `CIsoparser` subclass of `Isoparser` where one or more of the speed-critical functions has been overridden to call `C` functions, but the remainder of the logic is shared. This would be much more difficult if we were using a bunch of bare functions.

Likely we'll start out with `isoparse` being the only part of the official public interface, or possibly `isoparse` plus some bare functions for parsing dates-only, times-only and tz strings only. The fact that they are methods on some constant instance will be considered an implementation detail.

Is this really needed though? The library will automatically sleep for you only if you need it, with the reasoning that you'll call this method to e.g. get 5,000 messages (or all of them), so it will sleep, not that you might sleep. If you want custom sleeps you could call with offsets accordingly and sleeping manually. Or use the raw request. Sleeping a second is already optimal. Why would you want to specify another timeout? I have the same issue on Windows 10. It has to do with the use of a temporary file that is being opened three times, this does not work. In the `Map._to_png()` method:

- `self.save(fname)` means the temporary files will be attempted to be opened again, inside the `with tempfile` statement.
- then `driver.get('file://{}'.format(fname))` also attempts to open the file.

From [the `tempfile` module documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile):

> Whether the name can be used to open the file a second time, while the named temporary file is still open, varies across platforms (it can be so used on Unix; it cannot on Windows NT or later).

A solution would be to reuse the temporary file handle for both. Problem is that selenium does not accept file handles AFAIK. 

Can we skip using a file altogether by providing selenium with a html string? I tried this but did not get it to work quickly:

@abstiles `powerline` that is not found has nothing to do with `powerline` module. It is an instance of `powerline.vim.VimPowerline` class that is expected to live in `__main__` module. If

    python foo = 1

will add variable `foo` to something *other* then `__main__` module bindings will not work. But in any case you should not try to use bindings for old Vim because the very reason `pyeval` was added in first place is that other ways around are fucking slow. NeoVim is using msgpack over sockets and this is going to be way slower. I was not going to add NeoVim support any time soon because this needs a major rewrite of Vim bindings since

```
&statusline
   \/
pyeval
   \/
…
   \/
segment function
   \/
vim.eval
   \/
result
   \/
displayed statusline
```


is fine, but

```
&statusline
  \/
:python          --------msgpack-rpc------>  … > segment function
                                                \/
vim_eval         <-------msgpack-rpc-------  vim.eval
  \/
eval result (C)  --------msgpack-rpc------>  result (Python)
                                                \/
displayed stl    <-------msgpack-rpc-------  segment function result
```

(in all cases: repeated for each segment function which bothers to use some interface with Vim) is not and I do not need tests to say that this would not work.

The only alternative approach I can see is

```
startup notification  -------msgpack-rpc------->  powerline
                                                     \/
                                                     …
                                                     \/
                                                  segment.startup
                                                     \/
&statusline function  <------msgpack-rpc--------  list of things needed from NeoVim
  \/
computed list of things ----------msgpack-rpc-----------------------------+
                                                                          |
&statusline                                                               |
  \/                                                                      |
:python (+computed list of things)  -------msgpack-rpc------->  renderer  |    ----->  … > segment function
                                                                   \/     \/                    \/
displayed statusline                <------msgpack-rpc--------  cached result                result
                                                                                                |
&statusline                                                               +---------------------+
  \/                                                                      |
:python (+computed list of things)  -------msgpack-rpc------->  renderer  |    ----->  … > segment function
                                                                   \/     \/                    \/
displayed statusline                <------msgpack-rpc--------  cached result                result
                                                                                                |
…
```

, but as you see there will be a lag between when things needed from NeoVim are computed and when they are displayed. In this case it may though can appear that I can afford waiting until computed things are processed, so there will be no lag, but I am not sure. Rewrite is needed in any case.

There is also an alternative where statusline function constantly computes things and sends them to powerline and powerline constantly sends back an updates to &statusline when they are ready, but this is no different regarding how segment functions need to be rewritten and this variant is rather not compatible with Vim.

Some things in a list may be replaced with NeoVim events which is a bit faster.

In theory, including the tests in realease tarballs is good practice, but - in practice - it is not.
If you want to automate the build process for marshmallow I suggest you to download it from here

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/releases

instead of using PyPI. These tarballs include everything, source, tests, docs, etc.

If you are building packages in an isolated environment, using something like pbuilder, you can follow these (automatable) steps:

- install the build-time dependencies (using pip) listed here /dev-requirements.txt and here /docs/requirements.txt
- run the tests using tox from the root directory
- build the docs using /tasks.py
- create deb, rpm or whatever

A cool thing you can do is to contribute a Dockerfile for this project that automatically builds distro packages in a clean environment and put them in a volume.

The custom field described by @deckar01 only needs to be created once. Then, in the app, it can be used in place of the `String` field.

Agreed, this is far from perfect.

- The user needs to realize the feature he expected from marshmallow isn't there and a custom field is needed.
- The user needs to write this custom field himself, probably by copying it from a GitHub issue comment.
- In the schemas, the user can't import all fields from `marshmallow.fields`, he needs to also import from his own custom fields module. Cosmetic.
- This may have side-effects if a third-part library relies on the type of the field and can't rely on inheritance (for instance to create an OpenAPI doc, the field type needs to be associated with a parameter type, and `Email` field should not be treated as `String`, so I end up registering all my custom fields manually), but this should be a corner case.
- If the user wants to achieve this globally, he must subclass all the fields he's using.

Wouldn't it be better, however, to treat empty strings as `missing` rather than `None`. I'm thinking of the HTML form use case, for instance.

```python
  def deserialize(self, value, attr=None, data=None)
+     if value in self.null_values:
+         value = missing
        self._validate_missing(value)

```

Should this be used only on deserialization or on serialization as well? See this discussion about removing nullish values from output: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/229.

The idea is that the port is opened in blocking mode or with a timeout, e.g. 3 seconds. Then `serial.read()` will block if there is no data, so no high CPU load should occur. If the port is accidentally opened with `timeout=0` then this will not block an cause high CPU load instead.

I tried 3 different USB-serial chips, all create a `/dev/tty.` and `/dev/cu.` device nodes, and both fail to accept the timeout-less break-setting `ioctl()`.

Using `send_break()` instead is a workaround. Not sure where to report the driver bug, but it probably affects all such devices.

pySerial uses `ctypes` with no tricks, there is only one not so common thing in `__init__.py` where the different platforms are imported, these are selected by an `if`. So far i have not have any problems packaging apps e.g. with py2exe.

I would not like to add to add the proposed "magic" import line in pySerial, it would be an import that is not used anywhere which gives minus points on the PEP-8 scoreboard ;-) Maybe you can add that import to your application or find out why Nuitka does not pick up the dependencies in this case (maybe because `os.name == 'nt'` is not true while it scans the sources?).

@etseidler I would expect the polling solution to be slower in principle. as the asycio scheduler cannot schedule the task which is waiting for serial I/O when such I/O becomes available. In other words, the polling solution leaves the scheduler oblivious that the task is waiting on I/O.  In practice, I don't know how much difference it makes.

The incremental version uses a completely different (SMT) solver which supports theory combination, supports incrementality, and which is lazy in terms of float-to-bv conversion and bit-blasting, and uses different heuristics. I.e., it's fully expected that it behaves differently. Just a quick test on how the batch size influences the transfer time:
```python
In [1]: import numpy as np, theano
Using gpu device 0: GeForce GT 640

In [2]: X = np.random.randn(100000, 500).astype(np.float32)

In [3]: def loop(X, batchsize):
    batch = theano.shared(np.empty((batchsize,) + X.shape[1:], dtype=theano.config.floatX))
    for idx in range(len(X) // batchsize):
        batch.set_value(X[batchsize*idx:(batchsize+1)*idx], True)
   ...:         

In [4]: %timeit loop(X, 100)
1 loops, best of 3: 259 ms per loop

In [5]: %timeit loop(X, 1000)
100 loops, best of 3: 11.8 ms per loop

In [6]: %timeit loop(X, 10000)
100 loops, best of 3: 2.87 ms per loop

In [7]: %timeit loop(X, 100000)
10 loops, best of 3: 21.3 ms per loop
```

So yes, in relative terms, it is a lot faster to transfer 10,000 datapoints at once than to transfer them in mini-batches of 100 datapoints. But in absolute terms, you only save a fraction of a second per epoch. Feel free to investigate this further for more realistic data sizes (500 features is not a lot, and 100,000 datapoints is not a lot either), I'd be interested in more results. But for now I'm not convinced that macro-batches are a worthwhile route -- it makes the code more complicated and there's less GPU memory left for your model.

We created actions to allow us to track the provenance of data as it was processed by a program. The idea was that, by analyzing a chain of SimActions, we could reason about data flow. Unfortunately, SimActions are what we call a "first-generation design" -- they get the job done but they're very clunky and hard to work with.

The flow of data from reg or mem actions through tmp actions is an artifact of our VEX settings, not a base property of how angr processes data. With different VEX options (specifically, if we don't use flattened IR), we can have data flowing straight from a reg read to a mem write, for example. Likewise, this is how SimProcedures create actions, without any intermediate temporaries.

If the DDG requires tmps to function, then the problem lies with the DDG, unfortunately :-(

Yes, if you clear the vehicle.commands then you clear out all waypoints including the home position. When you upload, the bug reported here means that the first command you added will be lost. There are two solution - either add a dummy waypoint first or do a download after clearing in order to populate the first waypoint. I submitted https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/pull/260/ (not published yet) which is supposed to add some clarity on this subject. 

The implementation of the autopilot to use the first waypoint as the home position is a misinterpretation of the protocol. In any case though, a user should not need to think about this when using the API. Our plan to address this is to make the API hide the command - a user will have an api to access and handle home, and a separate API to handle missions. https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/issues/252

I guess it come down to what the purpose of `GangaList` *is*. Why do we have `GangaList` instead of a normal Python `list`? I can think of a few possible things:

1. So that we can provide a proxy list interface whereby real `GangaObject`s are stored but if you're in GPI-land when requesting items (`mylist[3]`) you get proxied objects.
2. Persistency. It makes it easier to put a `GangaList` in a `ComponentItem` and have it know how to store itself. Of course, we could just as easily have the serialisation code say "it's a `list`, serialise as a sequence" instead of "you're a `GangaList`, serialise yourself as a sequence".
3. Parents. I'm not sure where we're *supposed* to be here but I suppose it's good that the `GangaList` can automatically set the parent of all its elements to the `GangaList`'s parent. Of course, we could just as easily do that in the getter and setter with a normal `list`.

All of which come together to mean that I feel that really `GangaList` should exist simply as a way of proxy-wrapping a `list` so that `addProxy([1,2,3])` give a `GangaList`. And even then it's only needed if the list is containing `GangaObject`s.

> The createcrd CRD1 step used up my RAM. Is the createcrd idiom for projection?

This is just one example of how one might use coordinates data sets to good effect; if your disk IO is slow it is much faster. You of course don't have to create a coordinates data set and can instead make multiple passes over the data, although you need to make sure you run the `rms` command prior to `projection`, otherwise the coordinates used in projection will not be the same as those used to create the covariance matrix.

It would probably be better to add a `strip` command to this example prior to fitting/calculating the matrix/creating the coordinates, which will save a good deal of memory (also means you can omit the `!@H=` mask in subsequent commands).

Hi @Maghoumi - many of your assumptions are correct. The depth map is generated in the coordinate system of infrared1 (what we call the 'left' imager); therefore the extrinsic parameters of infrared1 and depth are the same. From this perspective, the process of extrinsic calibration between depth and RGB is no different from the Kinect.

The R200 is calibrated in factory and uses these values for depth map computation on the ASIC. The RGB sensor was placed on the board as close as possible to infrared1 to minimize depth/rgb rectification errors. However, if you compare the lens between R200 and Kinect, you'd see that R200 was size-optimized. In rather unscientific terms, the Kinect lens is bigger and probably better and might have more uniform properties that are amenable to the errors you're seeing at the edges.

The SR300 -- last time I checked -- does not have per-unit in-factory calibration and relies on an averaged model of the lens. 

> If that is the case, I would second a feature request for API support to change the calibration of the Realsense, since I think this is really an essential feature that this library should have.

We've debated it numerous times internally. The factory calibration toolset is huge -- easily 100k lines of C++ just for R200 alone. It's not maintainable as an open-source project, so it will never be included as part of librealsense.

> A tool to actually do the calibration isn't even necessary in a first implementation. I have done calibration for the IR1 and RGB image using OpenCV already, so values would be there, just no possibility to actually use them.

If you require better intrinsic/extrinsic calibration between left imager and depth, manual calibration and rectification is entirely possible and recommended. IR1 and RGB rectification is not performed on chip, so there's no barriers to using this depth/RGB mapping.

Of course, allocating and disposing the filters is clumsy, it was indeed for demonstration.
However, continuously allocating and disposing filters or videoframes in the loop causes a leak.
This leaks, albeit slowly, if you uncomment the Dispose just before the GC:

`
Imports Intel.RealSense
Module Module1
    Sub Main()
        Dim pipeline As New Pipeline()
        Dim coloriser As New Colorizer()
        Dim cfg As New Config()
        cfg.EnableStream(Stream.Depth, 848, 480, Format.Z16, 30)
        cfg.EnableStream(Stream.Color, 848, 480, Format.Rgb8, 6)
        Dim pipelineprofile As PipelineProfile = pipeline.Start(cfg)
        Dim profiles As StreamProfileList = pipelineprofile.Streams
        Dim colourstream As Stream = pipelineprofile.Streams.Where(Function(x) x.Format = Format.Rgb8).Single.Stream
        Dim depthstream As Stream = pipelineprofile.Streams.Where(Function(x) x.Format = Format.Z16).Single.Stream
        Dim align As New Align(colourstream)
        Dim aligned As FrameSet
        Dim decimationfilter As New DecimationFilter()
        Dim depthtodisparity As New DisparityTransform(True)
        Dim spatialfilter As New SpatialFilter
        Dim temporalfilter As New TemporalFilter
        Dim holefillingfilter As New HoleFillingFilter
        Dim disparitytodepth As New DisparityTransform(False)
        Dim filtered As VideoFrame
        Do
            Using frames As FrameSet = pipeline.WaitForFrames()
                aligned = align.Process(frames)
                filtered = aligned.DepthFrame
                filtered = decimationfilter.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                filtered = depthtodisparity.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                filtered = spatialfilter.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                filtered = temporalfilter.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                filtered = holefillingfilter.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                filtered = disparitytodepth.ApplyFilter(filtered)
                Dim data(848 * 480 - 1) As UShort
                filtered.CopyTo(data)
                'filtered.Dispose() ' *** Uncomment this and we leak memory ***
            End Using
            GC.Collect(3)
        Loop
    End Sub
End Module

`

Conclusion: There seems to be an issue with memory allocation when creating and destroying filters/video frames. The issue can be circumvented by pre-allocating everything up front.

The F200 multiplexes its infrared and depth images on the same UVC device, using a number of simple but nonetheless proprietary formats. Video4Linux cannot handle these formats without some slight modifications. However, due to a quirk of how Video4Linux works, an attempt to set the video mode to an "unknown" format will fail, but leave the device in whatever video mode it would default to. For the F200, this means that an unpatched system will always return 640x480 16-bit depth images at 60 fps, hence the "correct" behavior when you request only depth.

Another thing I would like to do:

For a structure with N sites, compute the the fingerprints (of length M) for each site. So now we have an N x M matrix of numbers of the structure.

Personally, I'd like to take the max across the rows (maximum fingerprint value for a single site), which will result in N maximum values for fingerprint. Then I'd like to take the average of those N numbers, yielding a single number for the structure.

Basically, the first step (taking the max of a site fingerprint) corresponds to seeing how "perfect / symmetric" the bonding environment is around a single site. Then, averaging across all sites, you get an average measure of this. For a very ordered / perfect material, this number should be close to 1.0. For a very disordered material, this number will be closer to 0.

But currently there is no way to start with this N x M feature matrix and then reduce it down the way I want.

Hey @syrusakbary, thanks for pinging me. I think allowing modification of internal functionality is a great idea.

The way I've seen this done in the past is:

1. Configuring which classes/modules should be used to provide functionality (such as Django's `DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE`, `TEMPLATE_LOADERS` etc). I think this can break down into:
   * Specifying a single class/module/etc which should be used (`DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE`)
   * Specifying multiple classes/modules in more of a 'behaviour pattern' style (`TEMPLATE_LOADERS`, `INSTALLED_APPS`)
2. Providing hooks into internal code. I've normally seen this done explicitly in the code which is to be modified. (Django [has signals](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/signals/), wordpress has [apply_filters](http://codex.wordpress.org/Function_Reference/apply_filters) and [do_action](http://codex.wordpress.org/Function_Reference/do_action), [used like this](https://github.com/WordPress/WordPress/blob/77e365efbf2e499e2ed11d29c101ea466cf1ceed/wp-admin/user/admin.php#L27))
3. Monkey-patching (which I'd say is best avoided)

After a little reading, I think the characteristics of a good plugin system are:

1. Easy to understand and use (i.e. both in code, and in documentation)
2. Loosely coupled. There is some central plugin manager with code code on one side, and plugin code on the other. Each side does not know or care about the other
3. Easy to debug
4. Have a mechanism for registering plugins (and potentially inspecting those plugins installed)

**Easy to understand**

I've been hacking around with Graphene for about a week now and I'm still pretty unsure of how the schema & type system work. I don't think this is the fault of Graphene, but rather this is just a project that is both inherently complex and lacking in documentation of that complexity (for now).

If the plugin system needs to be easier to understand than the graphene internals, then **perhaps the plugin API should be more verbose**. For example, providing multiple clearly named hooks.

I feel the code in the `CamelCase` plugin requires I knowledge of Graphene's internals. One must know that is it `GroupNamedType.get_named_type()` that one needs to override, which I don't think would be easy to discover. Neither `GroupNamedType` or `get_named_type` shout out to me as obvious places for this to be.

Separately: The `CamelCase` plugin also needs to check the type of `_type` for each call. I feel this is something Django's signals [have a solution to](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/signals/#connecting-to-specific-signals). (I'm not saying we should use Django's signals, rather that this is an idea to consider)

**Loosely coupled**

*I say this based on [a blog post from 2008](http://martyalchin.com/2008/jan/10/simple-plugin-framework/), but I don't this has changed much.*

The current plugin seems to not obey this principle as plugins require knowledge of Graphene internals.

**Easy to debug**

Plugins start to change code behaviour in unexpected ways. Even if *how* the behaviour will change cannot be made clear, I think the fact that behaviour *can* change should be. In the current plugin system it seems that we monkey patch a method to customise the functionality. I think this is a problem because:

1. The original code will never be called and it would not be obvious why to a developer doing debugging
2. There are no hints in the core code that it's behaviour may be changed

I'd suggest something like:

```python
class GroupNamedType(BaseType):
    ...
    def get_named_type(self, schema, type):
        name = type.name or type.attname
        # Either:
        name = signals.type_name.trigger(name, sender=GroupNamedType)
        # Or:
        name = PluginManager.trigger("get_type_name", name)
        return name, schema.T(type)
```

... or something along those lines.

Node that Django's signals will return a list of values, one for each handler, so this would need to change in our implementation.

**Mechanism for registering plugins:**

This is currently done by passing `Schema(plugins=[...])`, which seems like a reasonable solution to me.

I'm think I'm out of thoughts for now :-) Thoughts?

By definition, only GraphQL `ObjectType`s can inherit from an Interface, so is not possible.
But I think it could be reasonable to add other abstraction so they can both (`InputObjectType` and `ObjectType`) inherit from it.

Shouldn't be too hard, but I think we can do better than a make check time failure for this.

This information, "does the symbol I'm currently outputting documentation for have an associated comment", is already available, so it wouldn't be costly to warn at "make doc" time if that is not the case.

As you've seen with your clang patch, hotdoc has different levels of logging, and there is already a command-line switch for making these fatal. Each warning has an associated code, and the plan for the future is to have a pylint-style filtering system, both at project's level (in hotdoc.json, "ignore this warning code"), and at source-level (in a source-code comment, "ignore this warning for that symbol").

The only subtlety here is that when "smart-index" has not been passed for a given extension, then only the symbols that are actually listed in the markdown files metadata block should be warned about, which means we should not warn at symbol-collecting time, but at symbol "resolution" time (it's basically a second pass in hotdoc where we determine in which page a symbol should be documented, if any at all).

While that's clearly an improvement I'm interested in having, it is not a priority for me, and I think I'd like to make it conditioned to the implementation of the warning filtering interface I mentioned earlier, the reason being that some users may legitimately not care about this, still want to use smart indexes, , should be able to use fatal-warnings and shouldn't get noise in their build output if they decide to ignore this issue.

Summary of offline discussion with @lukesneeringer:

- We should not wrap *all* exceptions without any knowledge of what we're wrapping here.
- We should aim to preserve the original design intention of raising GaxError in general to indicate API errors, regardless of the transport layer
- There is already a mechanism for doing this used by [`api_callable` and configured by `config.API_ERRORS`](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/5eeb04a56eec983d2682879439625a994ddfe1b4/google/gax/api_callable.py#L418)
- We can reuse this mechanism here to ensure that we *are* wrapping API errors so that users don't have to worry about transport when writing exception handling code, without wrapping all exceptions
- We should include the string representation of the wrapped exception in the GaxError message so that it is recorded when users log their exceptions
- If the documentation of [GaxError.cause](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/de97b83b6ae31d93ca0ddfa62f48952c9658c7f7/google/gax/errors.py#L42) isn't sufficient or visible enough, improve it

> a clean case study for a problem that requires a parameter server.

I see a Dask parameter server as being useful in the same cases where the [Tensorflow parameter server] is used, because it also only works between machines. I see both of these as being useful when either 

* the dataset is distributed and communication is constrained (i.e., large models, low bandwidth or high latency)
* or there are few GPUs per node as constrained by cluster (which is true for most clusters – the top supercomputers have 2 or 4 GPUs per compute node).

I see a Dask parameter server as playing a data management role and handing the computation to the optimization or deep learning library. It would only handle giving the data to each worker, and communicating the different updates produced by the optimization process. I think a Dask parameter server would make it easy to scale to larger datasets, and would be similar but different than various Spark learning libraries (e.g., [BigDL], [MLLib]).

I do not see it as being as useful in the same cases as [Horovod]. I think Horovod is more performant than Dask in inter-GPU communication, but is harder to use and less fault-tolerant across multiple machines.

[Horovod]:https://github.com/uber/horovod
[MLLib]:https://spark.apache.org/mllib/
[Tensorflow parameter server]:https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/train/Server
[BigDL]:https://bigdl-project.github.io/0.6.0/#whitepaper/

> something you could ... develop?

I'll look for a use case where the Tensorflow parameter server is required and mirror that.

> Another possibly cleaner way of implementing this would be by first implementing actors

I think actors should be implemented. This would only be useful when the workers store state, which would enable fancier communication methods for synchronous optimization. For an example of the speedups of different communication methods, see https://talwalkarlab.github.io/paleo/ and select strong scaling instead of the default weak scaling.

We don't need this if we want only asynchronous algorithms because the model has to be sent over the wire. We could do this with synchronous algorithms too, but it'd unnecessarily waste half the bandwidth.

I believe I isolated the problem and found a fix. I put all objects into the same class:

```
dir_store = foo.bar
chunksize = (10, 100, 100)
da_test = da.random.normal(10, 0.1, size=(100, 100, 100), chunks=(10, 100, 100))
storage_obj = zarr.create(shape=da_test.shape, chunks=chunksize, store=dir_store, dtype=da_test.dtype, overwrite=True)

da_test.store(storage_obj)
```

Running compute functions on da_test worked just fine and I saw pods getting created and crunching through the data. However, when I tried to store to the Zarr object (storage_obj), that's when the pickle errors arose. 

Checked out the docs and saw a lock option which I set to False and tried different scheduling options:

```
dask.set_options(get=dask.threaded.get)
da_test.store(storage_obj, lock=False)
```

That seems to have fixed the issue. I think the lock option makes sense to me, but I'm not entirely sure why the scheduling option is needed for this to work.

I continued with more testing to see if I could make this faster. Something I did yesterday changed the time to run the import command from ~120ms to ~600ms. Which I'm guessing this longer import time is something like what @anselor noticed on his system. After further experimentation, I believe the cause of this slowness is not the code in `cmd2`.

I use `pyenv` and `virtualenv` to manage my python working environment. After the import went to 600ms on my MacBook Pro, I went to one of my other computers, a 5 year old hackintosh with 16GB ram, 4 core i7 and SSD. It was also taking ~600ms. I ran the following `cProfile` to see if I could see why the import was taking so long:

```
$ ~/.pyenv/versions/cmd2-3.6/bin/python -c 'import cProfile
> cProfile.run("import cmd2")
' | less
```

At the top of the output, `cProfile` shows how many function calls it made: 564,288. Upon scrolling through the output, I noticed that there were lots of calls in `pyparsing.py`. There should be no reason for cmd2 to be importing or calling `pyparsing`. I went back and ran the same command on my MacBook Pro, and it too had lots of calls to `pyparsing`. On both computers I also saw calls to other modules that we are no longer using, like `six`.

I decided to rebuild my `pyenv` environment and see if that made a difference. I did the following:
```
$ pyenv uninstall cmd2-3.6
$ pyenv virtualenv -p python3.6 3.6.4 cmd2-3.6
$ pip install colorama wcwidth pyperclip
$ time ~/.pyenv/versions/cmd2-3.6/bin/python -c "import cmd2"

real	0m0.141s
user	0m0.099s
sys	0m0.035s
```

I reran the `cProfile` command, and now there were only 49,275 function calls, 10% as many as before. The calls to `pyparsing.py` and `six.py` were gone. I went back to my MacBook Pro and rebuilt my python environment there, and saw the import time drop from ~600ms back down to ~140 ms.

> I'm wondering, would your previous suggestion on attaching the "untrackedness" property to the type itself solve this? What kind of limitations would be imposed on such types?

I think so.  Basically, if the type is declared untrackable, everything in the many-argumented `cast(...)` would simply see `tinfo->untrackable` and then force a copy, basically ignoring whatever `return_value_policy` was specified on the function.  Then it becomes perfectly safe to let the instance come back to C++: if C++ code tries to return by reference, that gets preempted into a copy.  It has its own set of limitations, of course (you can never return a reference to pybind), but it seems not quite as big as disallowing the object from ever coming back into C++ code.

I vote, at least weakly, in favour of a new header.  `stl.h`'s current self-professed (i.e. in the file header) purpose is: `Transparent conversion for STL data types`. It seems different enough to me to warrant its own header.

Starting with Certbot 0.10.0, you should be able to use `--pre-hook` for this task. Pre-hooks you provide on the command line will be stored and reused when the certificate is renewed (if you're using `certbot renew`). The only difference here is we don't set an environment variables before calling the pre-hook and it is only called once for the certificate, not once for each domain. Because of this, you have to write something like:
```
certbot certonly ... --pre-hook "move_htaccess.sh {domain}" ...
```
where you pass in the domain(s) affected on the command line.

We only need to define rigorously what would happen. That is to say,

```
[provides=[name1, name2,name3]]
```
could be expanded to

* case 1:
    ```
    [provides=[name1, name2,name3]]
    output=[expand_pattern(name1), expand_pattern(name2), expand_pattern(name3)]
    ```

* case 2:
    ```
    [provides=[name1, name2,name3]]
    output=expand_pattern(matched_name)
    ```

The problem here is that whereas it is easy for user to specify `output` in case 1 (if we do not choose case 1), it is difficult for user to specify `output1 in case 2 because it is not clear which pattern would be matched.

We can enforce the rule that only one name can be matched, and do something like:

* case 3:
    ```
    [provides=[(name1, name2,name3)]]
    output=[expand_pattern(name1, name2, name3)]
    ```

so when `name2` is matched, the whole group is matched....

I've been thinking about this and have come to the conclusion that it's beyond the scope of the library. We already provide the `InvalidAccessToken` exception - it's up to the developer how they want to handle that.

-- 

With regards to library scope, I know we've made some exceptions to the 1:1 coupling of library and API calls. As we've discussed, #112 falls under those exceptions. It's fair to ask why that's an okay exception to make and why this one isn't.  My reasoning is this: I think we should make special exceptions for helper functions when there is no other way to build those features with the current implementation of the library. 

Multithreaded support _cannot_ be written by a developer in conjunction with CanvasAPI without changing the internals of the library. However, a feature like this could easily be written by a third-party, as it doesn't require any internal modification.

I'm open to more discussion about this, of course, but as of right now I don't see a reason to add this.

def init_emb(vocab, init="randn", num_special_toks=2):
    # mode="unk"|"all", all means initialize everything
    emb_vectors = vocab.vectors
    sweep_range = len(vocab)
    running_norm = 0.
    num_non_zero = 0
    total_words = 0
    for i in range(num_special_toks, sweep_range):
        if len(emb_vectors[i, :].nonzero()) == 0:
            # std = 0.5 is based on the norm of average GloVE word vectors
            if init == "randn":
                torch.nn.init.normal(emb_vectors[i], mean=0, std=0.5)
        else:
            num_non_zero += 1
            running_norm += torch.norm(emb_vectors[i])
        total_words += 1
    print("average GloVE norm is {}, number of known words are {}, total number of words are {}".format(
        running_norm / num_non_zero, num_non_zero, total_words))
```

> The default policy defines context as the current thread, and manages an event loop per thread that interacts with asyncio. The `.copy()` are used because of how numpy arrays are handled. In Ray, numpy data is passed around via the object store. On the receiving actor (or task), from [this page in the docs:](http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html?highlight=zero%20copy#numpy-arrays)
> any received numpy arrays will not be copied automatically out of the object store. Since objects in the object store are immutable, this means that if you want to mutate a numpy array that was returned by a remote function, you will have to first copy it.

The log output is being logged to `stderr` then...
```bash
$ streamlink -l debug --stdout URL QUALITY 1>/dev/null 2>debug.txt
```

Alternatively, by using `--output` and writing it to `/dev/null`, the debug log will be kept at `stdout`:
```bash
$ streamlink -l debug --output /dev/null URL QUALITY 1>debug.txt
```

1. use windows,
2. want to download new products with the same query as soon as they are available without redownloading the older products,
3. use software that does not work with the original zip files (unlike e. g. https://github.com/senbox-org/),
4. don't want to bother with writing additional files to disk to keep track of the original naming/uuids.

I don't know if this is better than moving this to Stackoverflow, but maybe there could be a 'use cases' section in the docs, which always starts with requirements tags (e. g. windows, regularly download new products, need to unzip) and then gives an example implementation (such as my PR). This would help those who have this specific use case without bloating the Python API or the Command Line Interface sections but still keep this use case within the official docs. 
Keeping it with the original docs would save the user time in my opinion. At least it would have saved me time, as searching on SO is usually a step that I do only when I don't understand how and why something does not work from the original docs and alreay have enough insights to enter the right question/query into google, which takes time.

Good point, the first code (with pure protobuf) looks incredibly ugly and useless.

Let's try to figure out a compromise. Some options:

1) we give up 'shape inference to be part of the Schemas' and throw it in the gigantic file (i'd still recommend registry instead of if statements for extensibility)

2) we refactor IR a bit to separate the Graph-related part (connection between nodes and such) from pure attributes part. Note, that for shape inference purposes one doesn't need next/prev-like fields in the Node, just the Node itself. This refactoring would be tedious but one can hope to get a clean subset of simple in-memory single Node wrapper

3) we write a separate wrapper for easy access of attributes and shapes similar to InferenceContext. It pretty much needs to provide input shapes and easy access to attributes

From requirements - I think we should target the ability to invoke shape inference from the dependent framework directly, at least we should definitely do it from Caffe2 to reuse code. But I'd say if one wants to use it - it's fine to use the ir.h stuff.

Thus it seems that 1) is probably the way to go - i.e. giving up on my original suggestion :-\

Note that in `oauthlib` code the OAuth1 field `oauth_token` is named `resource_owner_key`.

I'm not an expert of OAuth1, but if we follow the RFC, you're right, it seems `oauth_token` is not mandatory and `oauthlib` should not throw exceptions for that. https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5849#section-3.1

It seems this regression was added during OAuth1 refactoring.

I noticed at least two places where a change might be needed: https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth1/rfc5849/endpoints/access_token.py#L132-L139 and https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth1/rfc5849/endpoints/authorization.py#L113-L118.
I'm suggesting to preserve the call of `check_request_token` and `verify_request_token` if `oauth_token` exists, and call another callback if it doesn't.

I usually try to teach an agent to only take valid actions, by giving negative rewards and ending the episode in case of the agent taking an invalid action. For example in the case of tic-tac-toe, if you try to mark a spot which is already marked, you loose the game immediately. 

In case of the lunar lander, I would think that if your fuel runs out before touchdown, you are going to crash anyways, right? Nevertheless, as was stated above, if there is no fuel, whatever you want to do, the engine will not fire, so the actions are equivalent. Still, I think the question is what you want an agent to learn exactly. Should he still try to fire without any fuel, because if he had fuel, that would be the right choice? Or should he learn to use his fuel more efficiently, so he does not run out of it? 
Even more, does your agent know how much fuel he has left?
